coredata.dc:title: Nanocarbon materials for lithium-ion battery anodes: Review
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelrahman, Nour S.
Lalwani, Shubra
Hong, Seunghyun
Choi, Daniel S.
Kim, Jang-Kyo
AlMarzooqi, Faisal
coredata.dc:description: Carbon allotropes are widely used as anodes and conductive additives for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) owing to their large surface area, high electrical conductivity, and stability. By far, as only graphite has been commercialized, with limited capacity and stability, it is important to explore other carbon allotropes as anodes. Our review provides a comprehensive study of theoretical insights and electrochemical comparison of various carbon allotropes, mostly as anodes in LIBs. The theoretical insights mainly express the interaction of Li-ions with each carbon allotrope and guide about Li-ion diffusivity and adsorption that varies with geometry, edges, doping, vacancies, etc. Electrochemical battery performance discusses the structural limits of each carbon structure and emphasizes adopting methods such as doping, hybridization, and derivatization to improve LIB anode performance. To the above framework of theoretical and electrochemical investigation of carbon allotropes, a third layer of recyclability is purposely included to offer sustainable approaches in selecting the most appropriate carbon allotrope as anode for LIBs. The recycling process of each carbon with the most relevant purification method is highlighted, encouraging the use of greener and more sustainable approaches. In the landscape of carbon-based anode materials, this review directs through theoretical, experimental, and sustainable aspects to optimize carbon allotropes as potential LIB anodes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon allotropes are widely used as anodes and conductive additives for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) owing to their large surface area, high electrical conductivity, and stability. By far, as only graphite has been commercialized, with limited capacity and stability, it is important to explore other carbon allotropes as anodes. Our review provides a comprehensive study of theoretical insights and electrochemical comparison of various carbon allotropes, mostly as anodes in LIBs. The theoretical insights mainly express the interaction of Li-ions with each carbon allotrope and guide about Li-ion diffusivity and adsorption that varies with geometry, edges, doping, vacancies, etc. Electrochemical battery performance discusses the structural limits of each carbon structure and emphasizes adopting methods such as doping, hybridization, and derivatization to improve LIB anode performance. To the above framework of theoretical and electrochemical investigation of carbon allotropes, a third layer of recyclability is purposely included to offer sustainable approaches in selecting the most appropriate carbon allotrope as anode for LIBs. The recycling process of each carbon with the most relevant purification method is highlighted, encouraging the use of greener and more sustainable approaches. In the landscape of carbon-based anode materials, this review directs through theoretical, experimental, and sustainable aspects to optimize carbon allotropes as potential LIB anodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rising global energy demands have severely disturbed the equilibria of nature, compelling us to adopt sustainable practices for energy production [1,2]. The ultimate energy production goal has been revised to affordable and clean energy production as one of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG-7) formulated by the United Nations under the “2030 Agenda” [3]. In this perspective, LIBs are leading in advanced clean energy storage technologies [4,5], owing to their high energy density, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and cycling life [6–8]. Existing in our lives for more than a decade, LIBs are still evolving in applications ranging from portable to stationary devices such as electric vehicles [9], aviation, and so on.

Commercially available LIBs with two electrode configurations consist of cathodes fabricated using materials such as LFP (lithium-ion phosphate) and NMC (nickel manganese cobalt) and anodes mostly as graphite [10–13]. Employed as an anode for almost two decades, graphite is now expected to deliver better electrochemical performance but is limited by poor Columbic efficiency and rate performance [14–19]. Therefore, exploring more carbon allotropes for LIB electrodes is necessary as they are known for their extreme stability, abundance, and cost-effectiveness [20,21].

Existing in different allotropic forms, carbon-based materials such as porous nanofibers (CNF), nanotubes (CNT) [22], nanowalls [23], graphene oxide [24], nanodiamonds [23], etc. offer diverse morphology and bonding flexibility beneficial for Li inter/deintercalation in anodes. Our review focuses on creating a framework, to evaluate best-suited carbon allotropes by leveraging insights from theoretical models and battery performances from previously reported electrochemical parameters. The theoretical insights highlight the interaction of each carbon allotrope with Li-ions mostly using parameters such as Li-ion diffusivity and absorptivity. It was interesting to note that the above parameters varied significantly with structural deformations (edge, defects, vacancies), carbon ring/bridge groups, pore geometry, Li-ion motion, heterostructures, and doping. The electrochemical evaluation of each carbon allotrope revealed structure-related limits and merits of each carbon allotrope as anode in LIBs. This section also highlighted the impact of hybridizing carbons with metal oxides and sulfides, heteroatom doping in the carbon skeleton, and creating defects, vacancies, edges, and functionalization of carbon structures on the LIB electrochemical performance. For instance, the modification of Li4Ti5O12 graphene foam enabled fast charging with a rate retention of up to 200C in 18 s, while the inclusion of hard carbon in graphite with an optimized ratio speeded up the charging (Fig. 1 ).

To the above framework, a third layer considering the recyclability of carbon anodes is also included for sustainable deployment of these carbons for practical purposes. While investigating recycling methods of carbon allotropes, each allotrope preferred a unique purification method favoring minimum structural damage and impurities. Further, this section highlights the challenges and electrochemical efficiencies of recycled carbons and encourages their reuse in batteries. Our review combines the challenges and insights on recent recycling practices and also aims to provide a holistic perspective on the current state and future potential of carbon allotropes in the world of LIBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of nanocarbon materials for LIB anodes

ce:section.ce:para: Graphite as anode for LIBs dominates the market with a 98 % share owing to its abundance, high energy density, power density, and long cycling life. However, considering rising demands for LIBs in grid-scale energy storage stations and electric vehicles, their electrochemical performance still needs to be improved. Typically, the energy storage mechanism of lithium in graphite anode follows intercalation and deintercalation of Li-ions leading to the formation of graphite intercalation compounds. With the increasing demand for fast charging, from the perspective of high-rate performance, graphite still suffers limited Coulombic efficiency and cyclic stability.

Understanding the dynamics of Li-ions in graphite electrodes is essential in elucidating the electrochemical behaviors for optimizing the efficiency and design of battery electrodes. The relative mechanism of Li-ion intercalation can be studied using different theoretical models and analyzing diffusion coefficients to accelerate the kinetics for fast charge. Especially, advanced computational approaches, such as density functional theory (DFT), can be exploited to investigate atomic-level interactions of Li-ion with graphite. A detailed analysis of parameters such as lithium adsorption energies, diffusion pathways, and energy barriers can shape new directives for experimental approaches [19].

A recent investigation examined the kinetics of graphite anodes in fast-charging LIBs and found that particle size, interfacial characteristics, and electrode design play critical roles in influencing Li-ion intercalation dynamics [25]. Remarkably, it was emphasized that in graphite particles smaller than 10 μm, the diffusion of Li+ at the interface is the limiting factor and can be enhanced by establishing a fluorinated interphase. Structural and electrochemical analyses of graphite lithiated to a static capacity of 250 mAh/g at two different discharge rates (0.05C and 0.5C) indicate significant variations depending on the rate (Fig. 2A). In Fig. 2A(i), the voltage profile at a rate of 0.5C shows an increase in polarization of approximately 70 mV and lacks the final lithiation plateau that is present at 0.05C. This implies that the lithiation process at a higher rate is hindered prematurely due to kinetic limitations.

The corresponding XRD patterns (Fig. 2A(iii)) show that at a rate of 0.05C, a predominant single Stage II phase is observed, while at 0.5C, four separate staging phases (IIV) are present simultaneously. This indicates that rapid lithiation leads to incomplete phase transitions and results in long-range phase heterogeneity. Cryo-TEM imaging (Fig. 2A(ii, iv)) further supports this, revealing spatially resolved nanoscale domains with varied interlayer spacings at 0.5C, in contrast to the homogeneous Stage II structure at 0.05C. This phase heterogeneity arises due to sluggish intercalation kinetics at high rate, which causes uneven Li-ion distribution within and between graphite particles. The coexistence of multiple staging domains and associated strain gradients underscores the spatial complexity of fast lithiation. Consequently, the rate-dependent differences in voltage, structure, and microstructure together reflect a kinetic limitation: at high rates, the reaction cannot entirely proceed through the staging pathway, causing heterogeneous, partially lithiated graphite. These outcomes highlight the essential need to optimize both electrolyte preparation and electrode design to facilitate uniform Li-ion transport and staging at rapid charging conditions.

To further enhance the understanding of kinetic and thermodynamic processes governing the Li-ion storage mechanisms, graphite nanosheets (30 layers) and nanoparticles (103 layers) were compared. The phase transition from stage II (LiC12) to stage I (LiC6) was thoroughly investigated using experimental characterization and first-principles calculations [26]. While thermodynamically allowed, this transformation is kinetically prohibited due to Li-ion clustering into compounds, involving three intermediate structures (1T, 2H, and 3R) before forming LiC6. The electrochemical lithiation profiles disclosed that the graphite nanosheet anode exhibited a capacity of 496 mAh/g at 10 mA/g during the second lithiation, whereas the graphite nanoparticle anode achieved 345 mAh/g at C/5 (~70 mA/g). Both samples demonstrated excellent long-term stability, despite the lower initial Coulombic efficiency for nanosheets due to larger surface area and side reactions. The results showed three redox couple peaks corresponding to lithium staging storage, providing insights into the complex structural transitions during lithiation and delithiation.

The preferred Li adsorption sites and energy profiles for Li diffusion along the periodic direction and the transverse direction in edge-modified graphite are discussed by Jiang et al. [27] They computed Li binding energies and diffusion barrier energies for graphite terminated with H, –O, and –OH edges. Among all the possible sites for Li adsorption, Ht3 for H, Hb2 for O, and Hb1 site for –OH terminated graphite have the highest Li binding energy. The Li binding energies for all of the examined adsorption sites in edge-modified graphite are higher than that of the pristine graphite and rationalized based on the DOS differences. Peng and group also performed first-principles calculations to comprehensively address the energetics and dynamics of Li vacancy diffusion near the non-basal edges of graphite, namely the armchair and zigzag-edges, at high Li concentration. They investigated intercalation energies in two representative states of charge (i.e. LiC12 and LiC6) and revealed that the edge has a decayed influence on the intercalation energies for Li vacancies in contrast to the pristine graphite. The edge effects become less significant with increasing Li concentration, which reduces the difference between the armchair edge and the zigzag edge. At high degrees of lithiation, both edges are expected to contribute equally to the uptake/release of Li, while the armchair edge will dominate at low degrees of lithiation [28].

The surface area and interfacial properties of graphite greatly influence the activation energy of Li intercalation and deintercalation and electronic migration in the anode. With a layered structure and low specific surface area in comparison to the other carbons, mainly edges and basal plane defects contribute to lower activation energy in comparison to pristine basal planes. The impact of edge morphology was studied on Li-ion adsorption and diffusion in graphite using DFT calculations [27]. The results showed that modified edges in graphite significantly enhance lithium binding and mobility due to unique edge states. Notably, Li-ions in such structures tend to migrate towards the O-terminated edges in graphitic systems showing the most effective Li diffusion (Fig. 2B). The above findings underline the role of edge chemistry in optimizing lithium diffusion in graphite, suggesting new directions for developing efficient, high-capacity anode materials. In addition, the surface area also affects the electronic migration, wherein, nanostructured graphite displayed much higher capacity retention at high charge/discharge rates in comparison to the conventional graphite [29]. However, the increased surface area also increased the contact area with the electrolyte leading to excessive solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation. This led to lower initial efficiency as a consequence of higher irreversible capacity loss [29]. Recent reports suggest nanocrystal decoration of TiC [30] to enhance surface conductivity and reduce energy barriers for Li-ion adsorption and conductivity. However, the increase in surface area should be optimized to avoid the contribution of excessive SEI layer formation leading to low efficiency.

Graphite, an essential component in anode structures, brings substantial challenges to LIBs. One of the main drawbacks is that graphite has a low specific surface area, which limits interfacial interaction with Li-ions and thus restricts the energy capacity of batteries. This limitation is particularly one of the critical hurdles in advancing batteries to deliver higher energy outputs. In addition, uneven distribution at a microscopic level, possibly arising from the process of graphite integration into batteries, can lead to irregular expansion and contraction during charging and discharging cycles and sequentially create stress that may eventually cause structural damage to the anode [31]. Furthermore, the internal chemical environment of a battery undergoes significant shifts during its lifecycle, which can lead to increased electrical resistance and corrosion, among other issues. The structural deformation cannot be directly controlled or managed by external electronic circuitry, making it difficult to uphold consistent voltage control. In this context, extensive research has been conducted over the years to address the issues while fortifying safety and preserving battery integrity [32].

To achieve efficient fast charging and high energy density, hybrid anodes were explored, wherein, the ratio of graphite/hard carbon ratio was optimized. Composed of graphite and hard carbon, the hybrid anode demonstrated fast-charging LIBs with high-energy densities (>180 Wh/kg), showcasing improved current homogeneity and reduced Li plating during fast charging [33]. Pouch cells with the optimized equimass graphite/hard carbon ratio demonstrated superior performance, retaining 87 % and 82 % specific energy after 500 cycles of 4C and 6C fast-charge cycling, respectively (Fig. 3A). This exceeded the retention observed in cells with graphite-only anodes (61 % and 48 %), addressing key energy–power trade-offs in LIBs. The improved cycling stability is accredited to the hybrid anode's ability to suppress lithium plating and maintain homogeneous reaction current distribution during high-rate charging. The sloping potential profile of hard carbon enables more uniform lithiation and mitigates localized overpotentials, which are common in graphite electrodes and often lead to degradation under fast-charging conditions. Collectively, these enable the hybrid system to maintain elevated energy density alongside enhanced fast-charge durability, offering a scalable pathway for EV-grade performance [34].

Graphite with silicon particles and starch-derived amorphous carbon was also explored as an anode focusing on specific capacity retention. This innovative graphite-amorphous carbon‑silicon particle (GCSi) composite exhibited a notable specific capacity of 448 mAh/g after 100 cycles. The interconnected architecture of wheat-derived amorphous carbon within the composite enhanced interfacial binding, thus improving electrochemical kinetics and addressing issues associated with volume expansion and pulverization of silicon electrodes. Notably, the GCSi composite released less heat during thermal runaway than conventional graphitic anodes, suggesting a synergistic effect among its components and positioning it as a promising, safer, and higher-performing anode material for future Li-ion cell technologies [35].

The aging mechanisms of graphite as a negative electrode (NE) in commercial Ni0.5Co0.2Mn0.3 (nickel‑cobalt‑manganese NCM)/graphite LIBs were also explored. Their study revealed that the primary degradation observed in the graphite electrode was due to an increased interfacial impedance (Fig. 3B), which contributed to a 52 % capacity drop after 1000 cycles [36].

Later, research followed novel techniques for enhancing the performance of graphite in batteries such as the incorporation of pore-forming agents like PTFE in graphite, which created micro-sized pores upon heat treatment. This modification led to the formation of structured pores in graphite electrodes, improving electrochemical properties [38]. The chemical expansion of graphite (CEG) was performed to apply it as a carbon matrix for depositing Si-forming Si@CEG/C composites. The large surface area and rich pore structure of CEG in comparison to graphene led to effective dispersion of Si with reduced agglomeration and nanostructure, leading to a higher capacity of Si@CEG/C (Fig. 3C). Moreover, the introduction of amorphous carbon also improved the initial cellular efficiency by isolating direct contact between Si and electrolytes [37]. Considering the commercial potential of graphite, a scalable method to craft high-performing fiber-based LIBs (FLIBs) was also explored. The fabrication involved coating graphite slurry onto aluminum and copper current collectors and demonstrated an impressive energy density of 85.69 Wh/kg and 91 % capacity retention after 500 cycles of the device. This industrial-scale production line maintained the fiber electrode's loading weight density per meter, encouraging its potential for various applications, including wearable technologies and wireless phone charging. Table 1 summarizes the compositions, major features, and performances of various graphite-incorporated LIBs.

Incorporating graphite into LIBs has shown potential for improving performance, especially in hybrid configurations with other materials, leading to efficient charging and impressive capacity retention over numerous cycles. Innovative methods have been investigated to improve the physical and electrochemical properties of graphite-based electrodes, and from a practical perspective, sustainable and recycled graphite usage has been increasingly important. However, challenges like increased interfacial impedance have been identified as degradation mechanisms, leading to notable capacity drops over time. In summary, while graphite offers notable advantages for LIBs, addressing its limitations remains crucial for optimizing its potential.

Graphene has been actively explored to enhance the stability and charge rates of LIBs due to its stable chemical properties and thermal and electrical conductivity [38,40,41]. Graphene is a two-dimensional monolayer sp 2 carbon with a surface area >2600 m2/g [42,43]. This honeycomb lattice arrangement of carbon atoms in a single layer has fascinating properties, including superior mechanical strength, high Young's modulus, extraordinary intrinsic electron mobility, superior catalytic performance, and optical permeability [44–46]. The remarkable chemical and physical properties of graphene have garnered significant interest, as they are vastly distinct from those of bulk graphite and diamond [47,48]. However, despite its beneficial features for electrodes, there are still numerous obstacles to wide application for LIBs associated with the structural integration of graphene within anode frameworks. Side reactions, such as electrolyte decomposition on carbon with extensive surface areas and breakdown of functional groups, have especially been found to impede practical deployment. These reactions have a detrimental effect on Coulombic efficiency, contribute to the formation of passivation layers, and result in the irreversible consumption of electrolytes, all of which are detrimental to battery performance [49]. Typically, graphene nanoplates are never used as standalone anodes and are always utilized in composites, coatings, and conductive additives.

Despite the application of graphene-based electrodes, possessing a theoretical capacity of 744 mAh/g, their Li-ion storage performances face challenges in practice primarily due to low binding energy and the strong Coulombic repulsion between Li-ions. For this reason, the effects of morphology altering the surface area were assessed on Li-ion storage [50]. DFT calculations found that introducing defects into graphene to increase surface area can significantly enhance lithium absorptivity, with the specific capacity rising with defect density. This observation was further confirmed in another study, wherein chemical vapor deposition (CVD)-grown graphene displayed enhanced diffusion of Li-ions in the presence of defects [51]. Specifically, di-vacancies and higher-order defects in the basal plane of graphene were found to facilitate Li-ion diffusion. In addition, a novel method for predicting defect population in graphene was practiced, wherein a correlation between lithium diffusion in graphene layers and the D/G intensity ratio was established from Raman spectra. The D/G intensity ratio correlates with the slope from charge/discharge profiles (Fig. 4A(ii)), indicating Li diffusion through graphene layers. A larger slope means slower diffusion due to Li aggregates on defect sites (Fig. 4A(i)). This electrochemical data can evaluate the defect population in graphene, a key material parameter [51]. One of the related studies discovered that the presence of Stone-Wales defects and voids (empty spaces/vacancies) in graphene could create local electron-deficient areas as efficient adsorption sites for Li atoms. This improved adsorption of Li atom increased lithium storage capacity whereas defects inhibited cluster formation [47].

In addition to defects, the diffusion of Li-ions is also influenced by graphene stacking configuration. Such as in bilayer graphene (BLG) systems, the diffusion coefficients varied notably between AA-stacked and AB-stacked configurations (Fig. 4B) [52]. The diffusion of Li-ions was ultrafast within the AB-stacked BLG in comparison to AA-stacked BLG, arising from the difference in the potential well due to layer stacking. A recent study also discussed a diffusion mechanism of Li-ions on graphene surfaces, identifying two distinct paths, labeled as GNa and GNb (Fig. 4C) [27]. In the GNa path, lithium moves from hollow site of hexagon carbon to the bridge site of the carbon‑carbon bond; however, in the GNb path, Li diffusion takes place from hollow site of carbon hexagon to top site of a carbon atom. Due to low diffusion barrier of path GNa path - 0.181 eV, it is more kinetically favorable than GNb.

Very recently, theoretical analysis using molecular dynamics (MD) and DFT has highlighted the potential of ψ-graphene in LIB anodes [49]. With its unique ring structure, ψ-graphene shows negative thermal expansion, high heat capacity, and strong mechanical stability. These properties are vital for preventing thermal runaway and structural damage in batteries. Additionally, ψ-graphene exhibits efficient lithium conduction, characterized by a low migration activation energy and a high diffusion coefficient at room temperature. With a theoretical capacity of approximately 339 mAh/g and a low diffusion barrier, ψ-graphene emerges as a promising candidate for high-performance LIB anodes, offering insights for future advances in electrode material development.

In summary, the surface area (defects, edges, and vacancies) and the interfacial properties of graphene as anode lower the activation energy for Li migration and enhance the electron migration. The above results from theoretical studies (DFT, MD) suggest high surface area and defect rich graphene is a superior choice for the LIB anodes.

Graphene, with its remarkable chemical, physical, and electrical properties, has emerged as a pivotal material in enhancing the performance of LIBs. Studies have showcased its potential to boost stability, increase charge rates, and provide a conduit for rapid ion/electron transport [53]. Despite the high theoretical storage capacity of graphene as anode for LIBs, its application as an electrode is still debatable due to challenges such as scalable production, a tendency for graphene sheets to aggregate, and concerns about electrolyte compatibility [53,54].

Considering the above-mentioned issues, 3D porous graphene was integrated with Li4Ti5O12 [54] (anode) introducing smooth conductive pathways for electrons and Li-ions and eliminating the need for current collectors, carbon black additives, or binders [55]. This LTO/GF electrode, as displayed in Fig. 5A, demonstrated a long and flat plateau in its potential profile at high rates, indicating excellent ion and electron transport kinetics. Additionally, it maintained over 96 % of its initial capacity after 500 cycles, showcasing its remarkable electrochemical stability. As another promising approach, graphene-incorporated Si/SiO2 superstructure displayed high structural stability with enhanced ion/electron transport rates [56]. The optimized Si/SiO2@graphene anode demonstrated exceptional cycling life (≈1180 mAh/g at 2 A/g over 500 cycles), outstanding rate capability (908 mAh/g at 12 A/g), substantial areal capacity (7 mAh/cm2 at 0.5 mA/cm2), and remarkable mechanical stability. When applied in a full-cell configuration with LiFePO4 as the cathode, the Si/SiO2@graphene superstructure exhibited a high capacity of 134 mAh/g after 290 cycles. These findings highlight the promising potential of the Si/SiO2@graphene design for commercial applications in high-performance LIBs.

With rapid advancements in graphene, it became clear that it could promote fast ion and electron transport, leading to swift charging of LIBs. For instance, the deposition of SnO2 nanoparticles on graphene improved cycle performance [53], and forming hybrid electrodes with Li4Ti5O12 graphene foam enabled charging in just 18 s with a rate retention of up to 200C. The excellent electrical conductivity and pore structure of graphene in the hybrid electrodes enabled rapid electron and ion transport [57]. A simple process was also developed to interlink graphene oxide and ethylenediamine nanosheets that acted as a regulator for N-doped graphene production and optimized Li-ion transport [58]. A unique 3 × 3 supercell structure with CC bonds forming both tetragons and octagons, known as T-graphene, was also explored. Its primitive cell contains four carbon atoms and aligns with the plane group p4 mm, was identified for its remarkable Li-ion storage capacity of 2233.2 mAh/g when used as an anode material [59]. Vertical graphene sheets were also grown on natural graphite using thermal chemical vapor deposition. This configuration improved the kinetics of Li-ion transport and could withstand up to 3000 cycles [60].

The Sn nanoparticles possessing high gravimetric and volumetric capacity were encapsulated within robust graphene tubes to achieve outstanding rate performance and long cycle life, despite the substantial volume change of Sn nanoparticles during cycling [58]. Due to high atomic ratios, the Sn@graphene composite faced chipping issues overruling its high capacity. For this reason, a 3D organic nanoframe as a graphene precursor was synthesized that facilitated the homogeneous dispersion of Sn nanoparticles in a two-dimensional laminar matrix of graphene nanosheets (2DLMG). The presence of graphene in this composite played a critical role in effectively suppressing the pulverization and cracking of the composite [61]. The poor cycling stability of SnO2 was also resolved by assembling SnO2 with an aluminum metal-organic framework (Al-MOF) and encasing it with graphene [34]. A three-dimensional composite structure, F–SnO2@RGO, was also evaluated revealing its remarkable capabilities (Fig. 5B) [62]. Formed through a hydrothermal self-assembling process, the composite structure incorporates a hierarchical arrangement of nanosheets, creating 1D micropores, 2D porous channels, and 3D macropores. This intricate design enhances ionic diffusion, resulting in high ionic conductivity. The uniform decoration of fluorine-doped–SnO2 nanoparticles on graphene sheets provided accommodation for electrolytes and facilitated rapid ion diffusion. The anode exhibited outstanding rate performance and cycling stability, attributed to its 3D porous structure, ensuring fast Li-ion diffusion (DLi = 9.87 × 10−17 cm2/s) and high electrical conductivity.

The role of doping in graphene structures such as 3D crumpled boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene nanosheets (NBG) was also highlighted in enhancing battery performance [44]. Apart from Sn, heterostructures of graphene with other transition metals were also explored. Such as with MoS2 to form a 3D graphene/MoS2 spherical heterostructure. In the structure, internal cross-linkage between nano-scale spherical MoS2 sheets and graphene oxide flakes prevents aggregation and exhibits structural stability [44,63].

The conductivity and flexibility of sulfonated graphene and polyaniline (PANI) were utilized with high capacity and ionic diffusivity of Fe2N to form a heterostructure anode Fe2N-SGO/PANI. This heterostructure exhibited an impressive reversible capacity of 864.1 mAh/g after 500 cycles. With an initial discharge capacity of 1204.8 mAh/g at 0.05 A/g, the performance surpasses the individual components. The enhanced capacity, volume buffering, and maintained conductivity during cycles are credited to the PANI matrix and SGO [64]. Similarly, nitrogen-doped graphene was utilized to transform unbalanced SiOC to form an N-graphene/SiOC nanosphere composite. The nano dimensions composite favored high BET surface area (34.42 m2/g), and low charge transfer resistance suggesting surface charge storage responsible for enhanced electrochemical activity [65].

These innovations have demonstrated exceptional performances in cycling life, rate capability, and storage capacity, surpassing previous expectations. Si/SiO2@graphene superstructure and hybrid electrodes such as SnO2/graphene highlight the remarkable versatility of graphene. Nevertheless, challenges such as scalable production, graphene aggregation, and electrolyte compatibility are areas that warrant further exploration. The continued evolution of graphene-based research, including doped graphene structures and three-dimensional composite formations, signifies the vast potential graphene holds in revolutionizing the future of LIB technology. Table 2 provides an overview of the compositions, major features, and performances of different graphene-incorporated LIBs.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as open tubular structures that possess high thermal and electrical conductivity with high mechanical resistance [73] and are either single-walled (SWCNTs) or multi-walled (MWCNTs) [74,75]. As anode materials, CNTs with interpenetrating structures facilitate enhanced conductivity and high capacity [76].

CNTs with 1D structure and high surface area with open ends that facilitate swift Li migration pathways and high conductivity. The first study on adsorption of Li-ions was conducted on short open-ended CNTs, including carbon nanobelts (CNBs), using Ab initio modeling based on DFT analysis. The study revealed that Li-ion adsorption energies and band gaps in short CNTs vary with the tube segment number. Notably, CNTs with 2 segments showed the strongest lithium interaction, highlighting their potential as effective electrode materials for LIBs. Li-ion diffusion analysis showed that while CNT walls restrict lithium cross-movement, open ends facilitate easy lithium entry. Ab initio molecular dynamics (ABMD) suggested that these 2-segment CNTs efficiently facilitate Li-ion exchange (Fig. 6A), with a predicted lithium capacity of 0.19 for LixC. ABMD simulations demonstrated that lithium atoms move to low-energy configurations within 9000 Femtosecond (fs), aligning along the nanotube axis and forming inner circle rings, which minimizes electrostatic repulsion. This diffusion reduces electrostatic repulsion and total system energy, supporting the efficiency of short CNTs for lithium storage [77].

A study by Rupp et al. investigated the potential of defect-engineered MWCNT-COOH as an anode material to address the energy density and charge rate limitations. They utilized one-step acid-treatment method to successfully introduce oxygen functional groups onto the surface, unzip the ends, and partially transform the ordered graphitic structure into a disordered and defected microstructure with voids/nanopores in sidewalls. In addition, Li-ion storage active sites were created, including oxygen functional groups, uncapping inner hollow cores, disordered carbon regions, structural defects, and voids/nanopores. The impact of these structural and chemical features on electrochemical processes of defect-engineered MWCNT-COOH, revealed multiple-active sites contributing to improved reversible Li-ion storage capacity of up to 855.6 mAh/g (at 100 mA/g) after 100 cycles. These active sites enabled various reversible Li-ion storage processes, such as Li-ion reaction with oxygen functional groups, Li-ion doping in defects and accommodation in voids, Li-ion storage in amorphous carbon regions, Li-ion diffusion into hollow cores, and Li-ion intercalation into graphitic layers. Moreover, defect-engineered MWCNT-COOH demonstrated excellent rate performance, delivering a capacity of 350 mAh/g at 500 mA/g, and a fast Li-ion diffusion coefficient of 10−11 to 10−10 cm2/s, attributed to the defected structure and functionalized surface with low energy barrier, resulting in fast Li-ion storage kinetics [79].

On forming interfaces with other active materials, the CNT maintain their conductivity and even during large volume changes. Such as in composites Co3O4/N-CNTs-CNF anode for LIBs, the DFT calculations (Fig. 6B) the N-CNTs/Co3O4 interface generated an electric field at the heterointerface, significantly facilitating charge transport [78]. This interface also supported good electronic conductivity, with more electronic states across the Fermi level, enhancing the electrode's overall electrochemical performance. As a result, the Co3O4/N-CNTs-CNF electrode exhibited a high initial discharge capacity of 1176 mAh/g at a current density of 0.2C and maintained 82.5 % capacity after 824 cycles at a 5C rate, demonstrating excellent cycling stability.

As LIB anodes, the CNTs face several challenges: (i) Limited ability of MWCNTs to wrap around active materials, impacting the suppression of volumetric changes during lithiation and delithiation, (ii) Need for increased production and lower costs, as reflected by companies like LG Chem and others. This expansion is crucial as it leads to reduced production costs, encouraging broader adoption of EV batteries. (iii) Unlike MWCNTs, SWCNTs exhibit a largely bundled structure due to strong van der Waals interactions, making it difficult to achieve uniform dispersion, (iv) The presence of metallic impurities within CNTs, these impurities, such as iron, copper, and zinc, can convert into metal ions in an acidic electrolyte, leading to the formation of dendrites on the separator. This can result in potential safety hazards, including explosions or fires. Therefore, effective dispersion techniques and thorough removal of metallic impurities are essential for the safe and efficient use of SWCNTs in LIBs. Therefore, the above challenges stress to research rigorously on CNTs to optimize them for their contribution in LIBs [80].

In addition to the above challenges, the low Li-ion storage capacity of CNTs makes it hard to reach the requirements of future batteries. For this reason, active components such as amorphous SiOx were introduced within MWCNT to obtain a composite free-standing, flexible film. The embedding of thin amorphous SiO2 on the graphitized and few-walled CNT generated an effective electron transport network with excellent electrochemical features, such as a high reversible capacity of 1600 mAh/g at 30 mA/g [81]. As the physical mixing of the microporous Si particles with CNT leads to serious agglomeration and non-uniform dispersion, adopting traditional methods restricts exploiting the full potential of CNTs. For reducing the bulk and contact resistance with CNT was introduced with pothole micro-sized silicon. The CNT here served as multi-point contact electrical network to resist the volume changes during cycling. The uniformity of CNTs was taken care of by etching the potholes in Si micro-sized particles. The potholed micrometer-sized silicon powders (PMSi)/CNT/C electrode displayed an outstanding stability profile (Fig. 7A(i)) and the highest specific capacity of 1803 mAh/g after 100 cycles in comparison to the other samples (Fig. 7A(ii)) [82,83].

A self-catalyzed vertically aligned CNT–silicon core-shell array was also fabricated as LIB anode on stainless steel using plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition. The vertically aligned CNTs served as an excellent platform for conformal and uniform Silicon deposition, leading to extremely stable binder-free electrodes with cycle life >700 cycles. The high mechanical stability of the Si coating was also demonstrated with >70 % coating efficiency [84]. To further optimize the structure for efficient storage, carbon-coated Si nanoparticles were combined with conductive carbon nanotubes, showing a 3D structure (Fig. 7B) [85]. Their findings reveal that this combination resulted in a stable SEI film and efficient lithiation/lithiation of silicon. The Si/CNT@C electrode outperformed traditional Si and Si@C electrodes in terms of discharge capacity and Coulombic efficiency. This enhancement is attributed to the synergistic effect of the carbon coating and CNT matrix, improving conductivity and Li-ion transmission. The Si/CNT@C exhibited superior rate performance and cycling stability, retaining a high capacity of 1106 mAh/g after 200 cycles, with a Coulombic efficiency above 99 % after the third cycle. Additionally, the Si/CNT@C electrode showed the smallest charge transfer resistance, indicating significantly enhanced Li-ion and electron transportation.

Apart from Si, Fe2O3, and MoS2 were also explored with CNT as LiB anodes. Such as to synthesize a stable LIB anode, cold spray deposition was used to synthesize Fe2O3/C composite integrated with CNT. Instead of attaining a physical mixture of commercially available Fe2O3 and CNT, the composite with smaller Fe2O3 particles (Fe2O3/C/CNT) was synthesized to obtain a shorter diffusion path of Li-ions and rapid charge transfer through CNTs with an excellent discharge capacity of 1598 mAh/g at 100 mA/g [86]. More recent studies introduced a unique cactus-like micro-nanostructure Fe2O3/C@NCNT as an anode material, achieved through in-situ self-catalyzed synthesis [87]. This novel composite, when used as an efficient anode material for high-performance LIBs, delivers a high reversible capacity of 994.9 mAh/g at 100 mA/g (0.1C), with excellent cycling stability of 944.7 mAh/g after 600 cycles at 1 A/g. Additionally, it demonstrates good rate performance, achieving 768 mAh/g at 5C, highlighting its great potential as an active and stable anode for high energy density LIBs. Their findings also reveal enhanced the solid–electrolyte interphase SEI film formation, favorable structural stability during cycling, and lower charge transfer resistance, indicating superior electrochemical activity.

A flexible, three-dimensional hybrid foam with graphene foam/CNT decorated MoS2 nanoparticles was fabricated as flexible LIB anodes. The wrapping of CNT on graphene foam facilitated excellent ion/electron transport, whereas MoS2 particles provided a high capacity of 935 mAh/g at 0.1 A/g [88].

CNTs offer enhanced thermal and electrical conductivity, making them promising for LIB anodes. Both SWCNTs and MWCNTs have shown the potential to increase battery life, storage capacity, and safety. From hybrid foams to micro-nanostructures and core-shell arrays, have showcased their versatility. Recent advancements have also emphasized eco-friendly solutions and structural optimization. Despite significant advancements, further refinements are essential for maximizing their potential. Table 3 presents a summary of the compositions, key features, and performance metrics of various CNT-incorporated anodes.

Fullerene is a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial that can be tubular (CNT or Bucky tubes), ellipsoidal, or spherical (Buckyball) in shape. After the first synthesis study of C60, the use of spectroscopic procedures led to the discovery and synthesis of other fullerenes ranging from 20 to hundreds of carbons. C84, C76, C70, and C60 were found in soot, while other fullerenes were synthesized by the flow of electrical energy through the environment. Fullerenes have shown promising applications in LIBs owing to their high electron affinity and can also be used as electrolyte additives to adjust electrochemical reactions [90]. Despite its benefits to application to LIBs, there are still challenges to be addressed for its practical use, such as high cost of fullerene, low specific capacities, suboptimal physicochemical properties, limited electronic conductivity, and uncertain redox potentials of pristine C60 fullerenes [91]. Early in 2010, a study found that the cost of purified C60 was around $4500 per kilogram due to the costly separation procedures involved. To put this in perspective, materials for large-scale water treatment or battery applications are usually expected to be priced in the tens of dollars per kilogram, not the thousands [92]. Although fullerenes such as C60 have long been associated with high costs for their production and purification, recent occurrences have made them many-fold more useful for industrial applications and economic viability, particularly where only small amounts are in demand. Traditionally, the Kratschmer–Huffman arc discharge method made the attainment of gram-scale amounts of fullerenes possible in up to 15 % yield, using equipment scalable to large-scale operation and a simple arc-welding apparatus [93]. This established the groundwork for large-scale activities, for example, of the sort carried out by Frontier Carbon Corporation, increasing the scale of production to 40 tons/year with projections for further operation at 1500 tons/year, decreasing costs from $4536/kg to the envisioned $100–$500/kg [94]. Still, the issue of purification was always significant cost driver, primarily due to the fuel for running equipment and chemicals for solvent usage. This issue has been recently addressed through the use of life cycle assessment (LCA) and green chemistry philosophy to rationalize a complexation-driven process for the purification of fullerenes using DBU (1,8-Diazabicyclo[5.4.0]undec-7-ene) and toluene. DBU is a selective complexing reagent that binds to the higher fullerenes like C70 preferentially, enabling the effective separation of the C60 without the requirement for energy-consuming chromatography [95]. Toluene, selected for the added advantages of low toxicity and high solvating capacity for the C60, greatly minimizes the chemical and environmental load of the process. Substitution minimized the environmental impact to 59 %, the cost to 85 %, and the chemical hazard score to 42 %, but also boosted the yield of C60 to 77 % [94].

Concurrently, more efficient routes, including resistive arc heating and laser irradiations based on PAH, have been investigated for further increasing the throughput as well as the purity [96]. Resistive arc heating offers continuous controlled carbon evaporation with improved energy control over conventional arcs, while laser irradiation based on PAH convert shaped hydrocarbons directly to fullerene-enriched products with controlled molecular yield. Another potential substitute is a semi-industrial AC 3-phase plasma reactor that operates solventless, utilizes recycled carrier gases, and attains close to 100 % carbon black-to-fullerenes conversion with up to 13.83 % yield, the highest to date at atmospheric conditions [97]. With the incorporation of real-time diagnostics and the absence of organic solvents, the system greatly reduces the costs of operation while allowing scalability in industry.

More critically, these technological improvements have been rendered applicable to functional, high-impact uses. As an example, Jiang et al. described the strategic incorporation of fullerenes into LIBs in the form of additives and protective coatings, instead of bulk electrode materials, to simultaneously minimize costs while maximally exploit functional advantages [90]. These findings collectively validate the industrial applicability of fullerenes, not through bulk usage, but through selective, low-dosage applications where the distinctive physicochemical properties of C60 are maximized economically.

Fullerenes with a nanometer scale structure have high surface-to-volume ratio [98], providing numerous active sites for Li-ion adsorption. The spherical structure facilitates intercalation/encapsulation lowering the activation energy of Li-ion migration in comparison to less porous carbons.

Fullerenes enable rich redox chemistry as excellent electron acceptors with enhanced conjugated π system and facilitate rapid electron mobility [90]. Although the thick stacking structure of the C60 limits the Li-ion migration and intercalation. To improve the specific surface area Shan et al. functionalized it with the carboxylate group to improve the electron density [99]. Therefore, to further improve the conductivity of the fullerenes, long range order porous carbons (LOPC) were synthesized promoting higher degree of electron localization transitioning from semiconductor to metallic structure. The group utilized DFT to analyze LOPC-C52 by removing 8 insulating carbons to facilitate π electron hopping with adjacent fullerenes leading to metallic behavior [100].

Wu and group [99] studied four C60 molecules with various functional groups, including pristine, carboxyl, ester, and piperazine C60. The comparison of these C60s elucidated a strong correlation between functional group, overall packing (crystallinity), and the anode performance in LIBs. Specifically, carboxyl C60 and neutral ester C60 showed higher charge capacities than pristine C60, whereas positively-charged piperazine C60 exhibited lower capacity. The highest charge capacity was achieved on the carboxyl C60 (861 mAh/g at 100th cycle), which is five times higher than that of pristine C60 (170 mAh/g), more than double the theoretical capacity of commercial graphite (372 mAh/g), and even higher than the theoretical capacity of graphene (744 mAh/g). Carboxyl C60 also showed a high capacity at a fast discharge-charge rate (370 mAh/g at 5C). The exceptional performance of carboxyl C60 was attributed to multiple key factors including complex formation between Li-ions and oxygen atoms on the carboxyl group, the improved lithium-binding capability of C60 cage due to electron-donating from carboxylate groups, the electrostatic attraction between carboxylate groups and Li-ions, and the large lattice void space and high specific area due to carboxyl functionalization. This study indicates that, while maintaining the basic C60 electronic and geometric properties, functionalization with desired groups can achieve remarkably enhanced capacity and rate performance for lithium storage.

The diffusion of Li-ions within the face-centered cubic (FCC) lattice of C60 crystals was first investigated using DFT [101]. The lattice, characterized by large interstitial voids, facilitates ion transport through a diffusion mechanism involving jumps between tetrahedral and octahedral voids, as shown in Fig. 8A. Interestingly, this study identified that the rate-limiting step for lithium ion diffusion is the intervoid motion, with an activation barrier of 0.34 eV [102]. This energy barrier is comparable to those in current solid-state electrolytes and electrode materials used in alkali ion batteries. Similarly, C24N24 cavernous nitride fullerene was also explored as an anode material using DFT [103]. It revealed that this fullerene can effectively host metals in its cavities, offering storage capacities up to 516 mAh/g for certain metal-ion batteries and 258 mAh/g for LIBs. The C24N24 fullerene, with its high storage capacity and favorable open-circuit voltage, is a promising candidate for advanced LIB anode materials, potentially enhancing the performance of these batteries.

The intercalation of lithium ions into C60 can significantly affect its electronic structure, leading to poor reversible capacity. To address these issues, obtaining high-performance fullerene-based electrode materials requires further modifications such as doping, hybridization, and derivatization. Despite these efforts, most batteries using fullerene-based materials have shown only limited performance improvements. In addition to these performance limitations, fullerene-based materials often struggle to match the energy density and cycle stability offered by more conventional electrode materials, which further limits their practical applicability in high-performance battery systems [90].

Several approaches were reported to enhance fullerene-based LIBs; wherein boron-doped fullerene derivatives demonstrate enhanced redox potential in LIBs [57]. When hydrogenated fullerenes are used as high-performance anodes, there is a significant improvement in reversible capacity [105]. This improvement is attributed to a novel approach to optimizing anode performance by controlling the hydrogen content in the active material. The hydrogenation process alters the electronic structure of the fullerenes, enhancing their interaction with Li-ions and leading to more efficient and stable lithiation processes, resulting in a stable capacity of 588 mAh/g for over 600 cycles (Fig. 9A) [105]. On treating fullerenes with polymers like polylactic acid and epoxy and nano-reinforcing to create nanofibers the structure exhibits improved microstructure, mechanical, and thermal properties crucial for LIBs [106]. This enhancement, influenced by nanofiller content and dispersion, makes these nanocomposite nanofibers promising for LIBs due to their improved stability, efficiency, and durability, which are key factors for the next generation of LIB technology. Polymerizing C60 using plasma thermal evaporation technology can also be employed to improve interfacial stability and capacity retention in Si anodes and Sn alloy anodes. Polymerized C60 with a self-dissolving superplastic matrix coated with Sn nanoparticles achieved high discharge capacity and retention capacity but is complicated to use on a large scale [107].

A nitrogen-doped C60, obtained by treating with KOH in NH3 resulted in porous carbon-containing pyridine and pyrrole nitrogen, proving to be a potent Li-ion anode with a reversible capacity of 1900 mAh/g [108]. In addition, an agent-free C60-pristine hexabenzocoronene co-crystalline anode offered improved storage capacity and electronic conductivity [90]. A unique nitrogen-doped C60@N-MPC formed by carbonizing a C60@ZIF-8 precursor enhanced the reversible capacity of Li-ion anodes [109]. Following a solid-state mechanochemical route, C60 fullerene was embedded into a ZIF-8 matrix, and upon carbonization, it transformed into C60@N-MPC. This modification increased the average pore size from sub-1 nm to 3–20 nm, improving ion penetration and transport. As a result, the C60@N-MPC showed a high reversible capacity of about 1351 mAh/g at 0.1 A/g and maintained 1077 mAh/g after 400 cycles at 1 A/g, significantly outperforming traditional N-MPC. The hydrogenated fullerenes (C60Hx) were synthesized to demonstrate the capability to absorb approximately 12 Li-ions per C60 [110]. A graphene oxide-based composite C60(OH)12 also contributed to the synthesis of an anode composite, pushing the charge capacity after 100 cycles to 1596 mAh/g (Fig. 9B) [111]. A cost-effective method was utilized to synthesize an elastic protective layer of fullerene-like carbon on silicon nanoparticles using natural polyaromatic molecules, that exhibited high cycle stability and excellent capacitance retention of 94.6 % [112].

Advancements in fullerene applications for LIBs have been significant, with an endeavour to improve capacity, stability, and functionality. Its adaptability in various forms, from hydrogenated to doped versions, has led to improvements in reversible capacity, safety, and battery life. While fullerene-based materials have the potential to act as anodes and adjust electrochemical reactions, certain challenges, like limited conductivity and uncertain redox potential, persist. Researchers have tackled these limitations through derivatization, hybridization, and doping, resulting in optimized interactions between Li-ions and fullerene molecules. Innovations like nitrogen-doped C60 have exhibited high reversible capacities, and fullerene derivatives have shown promise in maintaining the stability of LIBs. However, while continuous research is refining fullerene's application in batteries, ensuring scalability and addressing certain physicochemical limitations remain essential. Table 4 summarizes the compositions, major features, and performances of various fullerene-incorporated LIBs.

Carbon nanowalls (CNWs) are thin, vertically aligned sheets of graphene-like carbon, forming wall-like structures that provide a high surface area and exceptional electrical conductivity [107]. This structure offers rapid diffusion of electrons and electrolytes due to their vast graphene surface area, i.e. more extensive than CNTs. Compared with the CNTs, in the case of carbon nanowalls, the Raman spectrum displays strong and sharp D-band peak and shoulder peak, which are due to the small graphite domains and existence of edges of graphene sheets [113]. Advantageous due to their small mass, CNWs allow more Li-ions in a smaller volume for storage [114].

CNWs with large pores and vertical orientation exposing graphene edges facilitate large surface area and enhance Li storage reducing energy barrier for Li intercalation [115]. In-depth microstructure of CNWs was analyzed using quenched solid density functional theory (QSDFT) [116]. The QSDFT analysis, assuming slit pore geometry, revealed that CNWs possess a micro–mesopore size distribution with peaks at 0.7 nm and 2.6 nm. This detailed pore structure analysis provided crucial information about the internal geometry of CNWs, with a pore volume of approximately 0.2 cm3/g. This theoretical understanding of the CNW microstructure helps in predicting ion diffusion and storage capabilities in advanced material applications.

To explore CNWs for power sources, DFT simulations were used to analyze the effects of nitrogen and oxygen atoms on carbon [9]. The simulations revealed that nitrogen atoms could induce the formation of C-N-C bridge groups on graphene. Additionally, DFT calculations were applied to interpret X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data, showing a notable shift in the chemical structure due to the interaction of nitrogen, with a calculated shift of about 3.2 eV for pyridinic and graphitic groups. These findings highlight the critical role of nitrogen in altering the chemical and electronic properties of CNWs, essential for their effectiveness in power sources like LIBs. Although, graphitic domains in CNWs offer good electronic mobility, they are still limited by the vertical wall collapse and defects [117].

CNWs have a wide reaction surface that is beneficial for battery electrodes. However, their practical application is hindered by issues of low adhesion to substrates and limited durability and their stability as an anode material in LIBs. To enhance the stability and adhesion of CNWs, an interlayer of active material, i.e., titanium nitride and chromium, has been added between copper foil and carbon nanowalls [116]. The structural characterization revealed that a Ti intermediate layer was inserted between the CNW and the copper foil showed low adhesion to the substrate, as when Ti was exposed to the atmosphere, it was oxidized to TiO2. This reduced the homogeneity of the substrate, leading to rapid breakdown of walls, causing the substrate and CNW to be separated (adverse effect on adhesion and stability to LIBs). Although, on growing TiN interlayer on CNWs better was there with a small amount of Ti3O. Though Ti interlayer improves adhesion strength, TiN interlayer rather than Cr has an optimum interlayer thickness for adhesion strength. For the Ti and Cr layers, where CNWs were synthesized, the impedance values were very high at 129 Ohm and 122 Ohm, respectively while the TiN sample has the smallest resistance value at 97 Ohm. This can be explained by the fact that TiN is a high-strength ceramic-like substance with excellent electrical conductivity and corrosion resistance. Based on the low impedance and the interlayer thickness among TiN, Cr, and Ti, carbon nanowalls grown on TiN interlayers displayed superior structural stability with improved anode electrochemical performance.

CNWs were also combined with either copper or silicon nanoparticles on Cu foil. The CNW‑copper outperformed traditional anodes, while CNW‑silicon-CNW raised the capacity and reduced volume changes [118]. Another study also integrated 2D CNW with Cu nanoparticles for enhanced performance, or Si nanoparticles, and fabricated high-capacity, binder-free LIBs anodes (Fig. 10A). Their cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis revealed specific capacities and notable redox behavior over initial charge-discharge cycles and showed distinct reduction and oxidation peaks. This indicated the formation and stability of SEI and activation of anode materials, with variations in peak intensities highlighting differences in the performance and long-term stability of CNW, Cu-CNW, and Si-CNW composites [118].

Farid et al. also fabricated vertically oriented graphene nanowalls functionalized with Mo2C (Mo2C/VGNW) particles that demonstrated a five-fold increase in performance in comparison to bare Mo2C. The hybrid electrode (Mo2C/VGNW) displayed an increase in the Coulombic efficiency from 30 % to 90.4 % in the first 50 cycles owing to the dynamic effects between the highly conductive VGNW support and Mo2C nanoparticles [119].

When Ni3S2 with a theoretical capacity of 446 mAh/g was combined with the CNWs, the reversible capacities were significantly improved [120]. The unique nanowall structure of Ni3S2/CNW anode contributed to enhancing Li-ion diffusion and thus improving the efficiency.

CNWs were also combined with CNTs and carbon nanofibres (CNFs) to be explored as LIB anodes. The findings illustrated that the composite material offers optimal performance with a specific capacity of 64.94 mAh/g [115]. In another notable study, the combination of CNWs with CNFs enhanced performance as LIB anode (Fig. 10B). This synthesis improved CV performance, showing better reduction and oxidation potentials, and increased current density and durability than CNWs or CNFs. The CNFs/CNWs anode materials also exhibited higher Coulombic efficiency, up to 91%, benefiting from the large surface area of CNWs and the robustness of CNFs, which together extend the cycle life of the anode materials [117]. CNWs, with their low resistance and high surface area, were synthesized using a microwave plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition system. CNFs, known for their durability, were created via electrospinning. This combination resulted in an anode material with improved electrochemical properties, including a low resistance value (Rct of 93 Ohm) and high Coulombic efficiency (74% to 91%). The synergy between CNWs and CNFs offered enhanced charge/discharge efficiency and Li-ion electron mobility, showcasing the potential of carbon-based nanomaterials in enhancing LIB performance [117].

From another aspect, the potential of CNWs as a coating to protect LIB anodes was also explored. Particularly modifying copper or aluminum current collectors [121]. This study identified a lack of research on the interphase surface between copper and active materials and the SEI solid film coating. Proposing mechanical models of double interfaces, they coated vertical copper with graphene nanowalls (a.k.a CNWs) to decrease the resistance of the SEI layer resulting from amorphous carbon coating. The study achieved a notable rate change of 190 mAh/g at 3C, compared to previous records of 160 mAh/g and 90 mAh/g [121].

CNW with an expansive reaction surface is a promising material for LIB electrodes. While they face challenges in stability and adhesion, recent innovations have combined them with other materials to enhance performance such as using interlayers of titanium nitride and chromium or blends and combinations with other materials like carbon nanotubes and nanoparticles. These combinations lead to batteries with better capacity and retention. Table 5 provides an overview of the compositions, major features, and performances of different CNW-incorporated LIBs.

Porous carbon spheres (CSPs) present numerous advantages as electrode materials in LIBs, essential for powering portable electronics, electric vehicles, and smart grids. Their high chemical stability and electrical conductivity, coupled with a high specific surface area and connected porous structure, are key intrinsic benefits. The morphology, porosity, and composition of these CSPs can be easily tailored, enhancing their effectiveness in transferring electrons and ions within electrodes. Additionally, their carbon skeleton undergoes minimal volumetric change during Li-ion insertion and extraction, ensuring stability. These attributes have made porous carbon spheres a focal point of research for use as anode materials in LIBs, with various preparation methods and performance improvement strategies being explored, including heteroatom doping and structural hybridization with high-capacity materials [124].

The porous carbon spheres, along with high surface area also offer tunable porosity [124] that provides abundant lithium storage sites. In addition, the interconnected pores offer short diffusion paths, reducing the activation energy [124]. To further improve the electronic conductivity of CSP (low degree of graphitization) [125] in comparison to graphene and CNT many theoretical studies have been conducted. A theoretical study discussed the molecular configurations and binding energies between various CSPs and phosphorus, calculated using DFT within the Dmol3 package in Materials Studio [124]. For exchange-correlation energy and correlation interaction calculations, the local density approximation [86] and Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation (GGA) were used. These calculations aimed to determine the system's optimal geometry, binding energies, and potential displacement and energy changes. The binding of CSPs with phosphorus improved the electronic interaction and conductivity, mitigating volume changes and mechanical stress and strengthening the structural stability. This theoretical approach helped to better understand the performance of CSP-based electrodes in LIBs.

Following the insights on CSP-based LIBs, a study focusing on hierarchical N-doped hollow carbon microspheres (NHCM-12) for LIBs conducted DFT calculations to assess Li-ion adsorption and diffusivity [120]. These calculations revealed that pyrrolic and pyridinic N-doping, along with defect structures in NHCM-12, significantly enhanced Li-ion adsorption ability. In comparison to various graphene structures, it was found that the binding energy for pyrrolic and pyridinic N-doped graphene was notably higher than for other types, indicating improved Li-ion adsorption. This enhancement in Li-ion adsorption capability, due to the specific nitrogen doping and defect structures, contributed to the exceptional electrochemical performance of the NHCM-12 electrode.

A recent study showed DFT calculations to explore the interaction between carbon nanospheres and nitrogen (N), phosphorous (P), and sulfur (S) doping elements, as illustrated in Fig. 11A. The results indicated that doping with these elements increased Li/Li+ adsorption capacities compared to pure graphene [82]. Notably, P-doping showed the highest adsorption capacity. Additionally, the density of states analysis of the doped materials revealed metallic properties, essential for conductivity. The interaction between Li atoms and the doped carbon nanosphere surface led to changes in the DOS, highlighting the effectiveness of N, P, and S doping in improving the performance of LIBs.

In a recent study, DFT calculations were also used to analyze the electrochemical performance of hollow ferroferric oxide/iron phosphide@carbon spheres (H-Fe3O4/FeP@C) in lithium storage. These calculations revealed that the Fe3O4/FeP heterojunction interfaces have high charge density, beneficial for electron conduction and enhancing electrical conductivity [126]. The study also showed that these heterostructures have higher Li-ion adsorption energy, indicating a stronger capacity to capture Li-ions compared to individual Fe3O4 and FeP lattice planes. Additionally, DFT calculations indicated a lower energy barrier for Li-ion diffusion in the Fe3O4/FeP heterostructure, suggesting a faster diffusion rate of Li-ions, which is key for improved electrochemical performance of the H-Fe3O4/FeP@C electrode (Fig. 11B).

The application of CSPs in LIBs faces several challenges. A notable issue is their high surface area, leading to extreme reactivity with the electrolyte solution. This reactivity results in significant first-cycle inefficiency and capacity fading over prolonged cycling. In addition, the porosity of electrode materials, crucial for interfacial Li-ion diffusion, impacts the overall electrochemical performance. Another problem is increased irreversible capacity loss due to excessive SEI formation, particularly in samples with very high surface areas (e.g., around 1100 m2/g).

Since CSPs are likely to induce excessive SEI growth due to augmented interfacial reactivity, SEI engineering is critical to improve cycling stability. Recent research mainly focuses on two complementary approaches: electrolyte additive engineering [27] and SEI coatings [28].

Electrolyte additive strategies, including the addition of tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBATFB) to ether systems, have been shown to have a superior capability to create stable, fluorinated SEIs that are LiF- and Li₂O-rich. These engineered interphases efficiently hinder the development of dendrites along with lithium inventory loss, while maintaining ionic conductivity and the native structure of the solvation.

Conversely, artificial SEI (Art-SEI) approaches allow for proactive ex-situ modification of the anode surface through coatings that are specifically designed to inhibit side reactions and accept mechanical stress. These coating constructed through processes like atomic layer deposition, drop-casting, or chemical grafting can be customized to the particular requirements of intercalation-type anodes like graphite or high-surface-area CSPs [28]. Flexible polymeric or LiF-rich ceramic layers, for example, have been demonstrated to mitigate SEI brittleness, stop electrolyte decomposition, and withstand ion permeability without hindering kinetics. Of particular note is the effectiveness of the use of graphitic carbon coatings surrounding SnO₂ nanoparticles to function as a protective artificial shell that buffers the former to accommodate volumetric changes and restrict unwanted SEI growth, representing a coating-based strategy to promote structural stability and interfacial stability in CSP-derived anodes [29]. Hence, both electrolyte additives and Art-SEIs both provide complementary routes to manage SEI composition, thickness, and elasticity important aspects when considering CSP-driven interfacial issues.

Furthermore, previous studies have shown that solid CSPs often exhibit low reversible capacity and severe capacity fading, mainly due to slow kinetics and poor solid-state diffusion of Li-ions within particles.

Aiming to achieve optimization in the application of CSPs, various studies have investigated the structure and morphology of porous carbon, consequentially leading to the development of advanced processes for the synthesis of CSPs. Such as CVD was explored for the synthesis of CSPs by varying parameters like temperature, reaction time, and feed time to obtain resultant spheres, ranging between 50 and 1000 nm [127]. Such spheres were determined to have sp 2 and sp 3 bonds and were able to endure structural and chemical stability up to 900 °C in non-reactive atmospheres and 600 °C outdoors.

On doping with nitrogen, carbon spheres nitrogen-doped soft carbon spheres (NHCSPs), displayed a primary discharge capacity with commendable cycling stability, maintaining 1064.5 mAh/g after 400 cycles in LIBs (Fig. 12A) [128]. On Encapsulating TiO2 nanoparticles into CSPs, devised a watermelon-like structure for LIB anodes. This innovative structure exhibited initial capacities ranging from 496 to 25 mAh/g at varied current rates and achieved a noteworthy discharge capacity of 106.9 mAh/g after 5000 cycles at 20 °C [129].

CSP with wrinkled morphology was also chosen as a medium for loading red phosphorus (RP) through a vaporization-condensation process [124]. Supported by DFT calculations, they revealed that these wrinkled spheres can promote an efficient RP conversion due to their strong bond with phosphorus enhancing the cycling performance. A rose-shaped NiS2/CoS2@RCSs composite was also developed as an anode material for LIBs. Owing to its unparalleled nano-architecture and the bimetallic synergistic effect, this material exhibited phenomenal cycling stability (747.7 mAh/g at 1.0 A/g after 400 cycles) and impressive rate capability (500.9 mAh/g at 4.0 A/g) [130].

A recent study introduced a double-shelled hollow SiO2@SnO2@C composite for LIB anode as illustrated in Fig. 12B [131]. This method retains the hollow SiO2 spheres, avoiding the use of hazardous solvents for their removal. The composite demonstrated impressive electrochemical performance, maintaining a reversible capacity of 923 mAh/g after 340 cycles and 538 mAh/g after 1000 cycles, significantly outperforming traditional SnO2/C composites. This etching-free technique enhances safety and environmental sustainability and provides a blueprint for developing robust hybrid anode materials.

CSPs have become a focal point in enhancing LIB performance. By refining their structure and synthesis methods, researchers have managed to achieve significant cycling stability and enhanced capacities. Innovations range from the introduction of nitrogen-doped CSPs to the encapsulation of other nanoparticles, creating unique structures. Further advancements involve manipulating synthesis parameters, developing 3D structures for faster ion diffusion, and introducing novel electrode materials. These collective endeavors within the domain of CSPs not only optimize battery efficiency but also pave the way for broader applications in energy storage. The compositions, major features, and performances of various CSP-incorporated LIBs are summarized in Table 6 .

In LIBs, the prospect of CNFs has been particularly promising because of their extremely good conductivity and huge exterior surface area. CNFs are produced by electrospinning, followed by carbonization. For electrospinning, a polymer solution is forced out through a small nozzle at high voltages to form nanofibers which are then subjected to elevated temperatures to undergo carbonization. As such, conductive CNFs with high graphitization levels are obtained. This unique feature renders the CNFs possess other distinguishing characteristics i.e. large porosity, a greater surface area, and excellent mechanical properties that make them quite useful in numerous applications [135]. The superior electrochemical performance of CNFs in LIBs is due to their capacity to form a conductive network that promotes efficient charge transfer while sustaining structural integrity during repeated cycles. This characteristic is crucial to meeting the growing need for high energy density and long cycling life in applications such as electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage systems [136].

DFT and MD were employed to model the interaction between hollow CNF-GeP (initially using CNT and later introducing curvature effects) and Li-ions. DFT was utilized to evaluate the absorption behavior of Li-ion inside CNTs with various diameters, and MD was used to calculate the diffusion behavior of Li-ion between the GeP lattice layers that were coated on CNTs with different diameters. The schematic diagram displayed Li-ion diffusion into CNTs (Fig. 13A(e–i)) through defect holes in the wall, being absorbed near the CNT walls. Fig. 13A(j) is the schematic diagram of Li-ion diffusion into CNTs through CNT wall defect holes with a diameter of 10 Å. As the diameter of CNTs decreases from 250 to 25 Å, the distance between Li-ion and the C layer on the inner wall of CNTs decreases from 2.872 to 2.159 Å, indicating that increasing the curvature of CNTs can increase the absorption capacity of Li-ion on the CNT surface. Fig. 13A(n) shows the relationship between the mean square distance (MSD) of Li-ion movement group and the diffusion time (ps), and the D Li +. Due to the specific curvature effect of carbon fiber, the charge density inside the fiber near the surface is larger, which attracts Li-ion to diffuse from the outside to the inside of the CNT. Both experimental and theoretical calculations showed that the greater the curvature (or small diameter), the higher the charge density on the inner surface of carbon fiber, and the easier to the diffusion of Li-ion. Simulation results suggest that the nanofibers can simultaneously inhibit the growth of GeP within 50 nm and alleviate the volume expansion/agglomeration during the cycling performance, and improved the diffusion kinetics of Li-ion in GeP, especially for fibers with a small inner diameter and thin fiber walls [137].

A very interesting study was reported by Jeong et al. wherein, they screened the Li adsorption on various sites of the hybrid surface/interface of NiMnS-2.0(110)/G (as G was considered equivalent to CNF). Such as carbon-top, Hollow, Mn-top, and S-top were shown in Fig. 13B(a–d), with adsorption energies of −1.648, −3.477, −2.917, and −3.954 eV, respectively. The S-top site (Fig. 13B(d)) exhibited the most favorable Li adsorption compared to the other sites. Notably, an increasing trend in Li adsorption energies was observed between the graphene and NiMnS-2.0(1 1 0) surfaces compared to adsorption on top of the graphene surface. Thus, the computational-study confirmed that the hybrid NiMnS-2.0(1 1 0)/G surface provides enhanced Li adsorption energies and improved conductivity of the material. They also identified the Li transportation path i.e. Mn-top to S-hollow site and Mn-top to S-top site within the NiMnS-2.0 (1 1 0)/G surface. Both the sites were within the range of the diffusion energy barrier for the anode materials <0.8 eV making them suitable for fast discharge rate (Fig. 13B) [138].

A similar study by the same group using NiCo2O4(311)- G surface was performed. From the various possible sites of the NiCo2O4(311)– G hybrid surface for the adsorption of Li atom, sites such as carbon-top, CoO-bridge, and Ni-top were observed. Their adsorption energies were −2.267, −4.794, and −4.225eV, respectively confirming that the most favorable Li adsorption site was CoO-bridge site in comparison to others [139].

Electrospun CNFs, prepared at various temperatures of 600 °C, 800 °C, and 1000 °C, exhibited different electrochemical performances. Particularly, CNF-800 displayed high reversible capacity and low resistance, maintaining good electrochemical kinetics despite volume expansion at the edges. Impedance studies highlighted the superior performance of CNF-800 compared to CNF-600 and CNF-1000, demonstrating well-sustained electronic contact with the electrolyte and good conductivity. In this case, carbon nanofibers enhanced battery performance by offering a stable and conductive framework that supported efficient lithium-ion transport and minimum impedance (Fig. 14A) [141]. Research into the electrochemical properties of coal-based carbon fibers (CFs) found that coal tar pitch-derived carbon fibers (CTP-CFs) exhibited better rate properties and cycling stability relative to lignin-based carbon fibers (L-CFs) and hydrothermally activated carbon fibers (HA-CFs). Specifically, CTP-CFs achieved a reversible capacity of 408.1 mAh/g after 200 cycles at a current density of 100 mA per gram (mA/g), retaining 89.1% of their initial capacity. This performance can be attributed to the larger average fiber diameter, higher microporosity, and increased pyrrole nitrogen content of CTP-CFs, which provide additional active sites for lithium-ion insertion and help mitigate strain during lithiation and delithiation processes.
