These carbon fibers were crucial in enhancing battery performance by offering structural stability and a high surface area for lithium-ion storage [142].

To emphasize high-rate capability and cycling life, single-layered MoS2-CNF was used in both lithiation and sodiation processes [143]. For lithium storage, the composite retained a capacity of approximately 661 mAh/g after 1000 cycles at 10 A/g. The unique structure of MoS2 nanoplates in CNFs provided an optimized environment for ion storage, thereby reducing voltage gaps and enhancing kinetics. In this respect, MoS2-CNF composites are highly efficient energy storage materials that have demonstrated the potential for high capacities and excellent stability towards next-generation batteries. The composite materials of MoS2 in a single carbon nanofiber appear to have outstanding cycling stability and rate performance. The electrode held a capacity of 661 mAh/g even after passing >1000 cycles at the current density of 10 A/g. This unique type of nanostructured material allows for better charge storage and faster ion transport by reducing voltage difference. As a result, these energy-storage systems can be regarded as highly efficient for MoS2-CNF composites, which are capable of being characterized by high capacities and good stabilities typical for next-generation batteries [144]. On incorporating MnCo2Ox nanoparticles into CNFs as anode excellent lithium storage performance was achieved with a stable capacity of 780.3 milliampere hours per gram (mAh/g) at 200 milliamperes per gram (mA/g) after 250 cycles. The amorphous structure and defective carbon nanofiber matrices facilitated ion diffusion, increased ion storage sites, and mitigated volume changes during ion insertion/extraction processes [145]. Composites of CNFs were also formed with iron oxide (Fe3O4@CNFs) demonstrating improved cycling performance and stability in comparison to iron oxide nanofibers (FexOy@NFs). The initial discharge capacity of Fe3O4@CNFs was 894 mAh/g, maintained over 150 cycles with minimal capacity fading. This superior performance is attributed to the conductive carbon matrix, which enhanced overall electrical conductivity and facilitated better charge transfer during cycling [135].

CNFs were also combined with SnOx to form composites at 750 °C. The composite delivered a discharge capacity of 722 mAh/g in the 20th cycle and 674 mAh/g after one hundred cycles. This high-capacity retention and stability derived from the ultrafine SnOx particles embedded within CNFs lead to high electrical conductivity forming a conductive matrix that improved charge transfer and enhanced electrode performance [136]. The silicon/carbon nanofiber (Si/CNF) composites also achieved a specific capacity of up to 3000 mAh/g and displayed stable cycle life. The silicon nanoparticles addressed the issues related to volume expansion and pulverization to improve the overall performance of CNFs in lithium-ion batteries. However, the carbon nanofibers offered flexibility and conductivity for an open channel for silicon to expand without breakage upon cycling [147].

The silicon/carbon nanofiber/carbon nanotube (Si/CNFs/CNTs) exhibited a significant initial discharge capacity due to forming a SEI film during the preliminary cycles. However, the presence of CNTs in the composite enhanced electrical conductivity and structural stability, leading to improved cyclic performance and capacity retention over multiple charge/discharge cycles as shown in Fig. 14B [146]. Another unique composite structure of SnS-C/NS@CNFs significantly enhanced the charge-discharge cycles of LIBs. The initial discharge-specific capacity was 1328.1 mAh/g, while the charge-specific capacity was 950.7 mAh/g. The specific capacity remained stable over numerous cycles, attributable to the protective porous carbon structure and the effective limitation of volume changes of SnS nanoparticles within the CNFs [148]. The encapsulation of silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) via electrospinning, followed by carbonization also improved the performance of LIB anode materials. This study utilized silicon-containing polyester as an encapsulating material and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) as a spinning additive. The encapsulated SiNPs were carbonized at 755 °C to form a Si-O-C material. The resulting Si@C-SiNF-5 and Si@C-SiNF-10 fibers exhibited enhanced structural stability and improved electrochemical performance. The cycling stability of the encapsulated SiNPs was significantly increased, maintaining a capacity over 500 mAh/g after 30 cycles, compared to the rapid capacity degradation of unencapsulated SiNPs.

As a hybrid material, N-doped helical structured CNF-CNT/TiO2 displayed significant improvement, i.e., during 100 cycles at 75 mA/g, it achieved a stable discharge-charge capacity of 641/304 mAh/g and then reached 320/316 mAh/g. The hierarchical growth of CNFs with CNTs around them optimized the ion diffusion path and electron transport, reducing internal resistance and enhancing rate capability. While the increased surface area of TiO2 nanoparticles provided active sites where Li-ions could be adsorbed while the carbon matrix facilitated charge transfer to take place efficiently [149].

Overall, the inclusion of active materials, such as silicon or even metal oxides in the CNF matrix, has enhanced their electrochemical properties substantially. Si-containing CNFs showed enhanced Li-ion storage capacity resulting from the encapsulation of SiNPs into the carbon matrix, that curbs the volume expansion and pulverization. Likewise, the integration of metal oxides such as MnCo2Ox in CNFs enhances lithium storage performance by increasing the active sites for ion diffusion and overall electrode conductivity. CNFs with large surface area and porous nature allow efficient transport of Li-ions while reducing impedance, thus enhancing rate capability and cycling stability of anode materials. For instance, CNF-800 showed high reversible capacity and low resistance, maintaining good electrochemical kinetics despite edge effect volume expansion. Moreover, LIBs based on SnS-C/NS@CNFs with unique structures, including charge-discharge cycles, significantly outperform those using other tin-based anodes, confirming the importance of maintaining constant battery performance through CNFs.

Meanwhile, CNFs carbonized at 1000 °C (PAN-1000) showed a lower D/G ratio (which is the intensity ratio of the disorder-induced D-band to the graphitic G-band in Raman spectra (i.e. structural disorder versus graphitization) and sharp XRD peaks indicative of enhanced graphitization. However, it still displayed limited porosity (BET: 40.6 m2/g) and poor electrochemical capacity (~173 mAh/g) [30]. Although, at 400 °C a defect-rich, porous structures (up to 786.2 m2/g) with moderate graphitization, displayed improved reversible capacity of ~473.5 mAh/g [31]. Similar observations were seen in CNFs treated at 800 °C, which displayed an ideal balance of graphitization (D/G = 0.785), moderate surface area (260 m2/g), and pore size (~1.88 nm), with a long-term capacity (460 mAh/g after 550 cycles) [19]. In contrast, using a fixed carbonization at 700 °C, one study highlighted that in situ air oxidation at 250 °C preserved carbon content (35.6 %), provided mesopores (~7 nm), and improved electrochemical performance (780.3 mAh/g after 250 cycles) due to its amorphous, defect-rich structure (D/G = 2.07) [32]. In another study, carbonization at 500 °C yielded a low BET area (151 m2/g) and moderate graphitization (D/G = 0.83), still supporting high cycling stability (773.6 mAh/g after 300 cycles), likely due to tight Fe₃O₄‑carbon integration [33]. Comparatively, other studies utilizing 800 °C carbonization reported CNFs with higher surface areas (up to 179.6 m2/g) and improved graphitization (D/G ~ 0.82), enabled by additional CNT decoration and dual-network carbon structures [34,35]. These configurations achieved stable cycling and superior capacities (639.9 mAh/g after 100 cycles) [34,35]. Lastly, carbonization at 650 °C led to N/S-doped CNFs with high defect density (D/G = 1.7) and a core–shell SnS-C structure. Although the BET surface area was lower (16.8 m2/g), the electrode sustained a remarkable 455.8 mAh/g after 1000 cycles, underscoring the importance of hierarchical pore structure and defect engineering over crystallinity alone [36]. Furthermore, it was shown that even at 900 °C, the inclusion of candle soot (CS) nanoparticles introduced structural defects (D/G ~ 1.02–1.41) and moderate surface area (~30 m2/g), enabling excellent performance (865 mAh/g after 400 cycles at 1 A/g) due to enhanced conductivity and sulfur anchoring [37]. Likewise, a TiO₂-embedded, nitrogen-doped CNF–CNT hybrid carbonized at 750 °C could achieve a very high surface area (295 m2/g) and stable capacity (244 mAh/g after 100 cycles), reinforcing that mesoporosity, conductive networks, and dopant-induced charge pathways are more critical than crystallinity alone [38]. Collectively, these findings affirm that while graphitization enhances conductivity, it is the preservation or engineering of mesoporosity and defect density at moderate carbonization temperatures (600–800 °C) that most effectively optimize lithium storage in CNF-based anodes. Therefore, the interplay between temperature, structure, and electrochemical behavior must be considered holistically, rather than relying solely on graphitization as a predictor of performance. Table 7 provides an overview of the compositions, major features, and performances of different CNF-incorporated LIBs.

Graphynes are 2D materials composed of sp and sp 2 bonded carbon arranged in a crystal lattice with hexagons connected by acetylenic bonds, leading to creating a natural band gap and diverse electronic properties [53]. Graphyne (GY) and graphdiyne (GDY) are unique carbon allotropes with potential applications in LIBs. Unlike a flat layer of hexagonal graphene, graphyne connects these hexagons with a single linear acetylenic chain, while GDY uses two chains [153,154]. Both are semiconductive, with band gaps around 0.5–0.6 eV, and feature high carrier mobility essential for conductivity. GDY offers a 0.46 eV band gap and high electron mobility, whereas graphyne is softer than graphene, with notable in-plane stiffness. These materials exceed graphite's lithium intercalation limits, accommodating higher lithium densities (LiC4 in graphyne and LiC3 in graphdiyne), making them promising for improving LIB performance.

DFT calculations analyzed the interaction between γ-GY and LTO in LIBs (Fig. 15A) [155]. The analysis indicated that the LTO/GY composite significantly improves electronic conductivity due to enhanced electron cloud rearrangement. Moreover, the study showed that γ-GY reduces the interfacial Li-ion diffusion barrier, facilitating faster Li-ion migration and improved charge transfer. This leads to superior lithium-storage capability in the LTO/GY composite, highlighting the effectiveness of γ-GY in enhancing LIBs performance.

To analyze the impact of internal and external fracture surfaces (FSs) of GDY on anode performance of LIBs, first-principles calculations were utilized [156]. The investigation revealed that FSs have stronger attractions to Li atoms, with Li binding energy on internal FSs ranging from −2.6 to −1.95 eV, significantly lower than −1.12 eV for perfect GDY. This results in higher open-circuit voltages for FSs, from 2.6 to 1.31 V, compared to 0.74 V for perfect GDY. Additionally, the diffusion barriers for Li atoms on FSs were found to be 0.42 eV and 0.47 eV for internal and external FSs (Fig. 15B), respectively, both lower than the 0.638 eV for intercalation sites. The FSs also show nonzero values at the Fermi level in the density of states, indicating enhanced electronic transport.

Meanwhile, the capabilities of N-, P-, and as-doped GDYs as anode were explored in LIBs [157]. The findings revealed impressive capacities for these doped GDYs: 2209 mAh/g for N-doped, 2031 mAh/g for P-doped, and 1681 mA h/g for as-doped GDYs, significantly surpassing the capacity of undoped GDY (Fig. 15C). The Li-ion diffusion barriers on these doped GDYs were comparable to those on undoped GDY, with values of 0.28 eV for N-doped, 0.27 eV for P-doped, and 0.25 eV for as-doped GDYs. These barriers are relatively lower than the 0.47–0.48 eV observed for Li-ion diffusion on graphene, indicating the superior rate performance of doped GDYs in LIBs.

Nitrogen-doped graphdiyne (N-GDY) displayed excellent abilities in suppressing the formation of dendrites on lithium metal anodes. Synthesized by growing N-GDY nanowalls on copper foam skeletons using low-temperature wet chemistry followed by thermal nitridation. In comparison to CVD-derived carbon materials, GDY's 2D layered structure made up of sp and sp 2-hybridized carbon atoms provided better structural control and synthesis conditions. The lithiophilicity of N-GDY became apparent from the DFT simulations through nitrogen doping at five positions in the graphdiyne lattice. The main nitrogen species are N(g) and N(d), which show stronger affinity towards Li atoms with binding energies of −3.18 eV and − 3.59 eV, respectively, than pristine graphdiyne (−2.41 eV). Fast movement of Li-ions during plating is allowed due to the presence of layered structures in N-GDY nanowalls along with 18‑carbon hexagonal nano-cavities, promoting uniform and dendrite-free Li deposition. The N-GDY successfully inhibits dendrite growth leading to nucleation overpotential of 13 mV and plating/stripping coulombic efficiency above 99 %. Using a carbon-based membrane is considered a promising candidate for LIB electrode protection.

GDY nanowalls were first synthesized with precise stripe arrays by controlling copper ion numbers via a modified Glaser-Hay coupling reaction. The structures possessed exceptional features, such as large specific surface area, asymmetric π conjugations, and porous channel, making it a highly promising anode material for LIBs, confirming its high specific capacity [19,158]. The performance of GDY was also assessed with balanced doping of F and H, which was achieved via cross-coupling reaction as illustrated in Fig. 16A(i). The doping of F in the structure significantly improved the wettability towards the organic electrolyte, accelerating the transmission and stability. However, H substitution was done to achieve an effective combination of Li-ions to improve the Li storage capacity to a remarkably high capacity of 2050 mAh/g at 50 mA/g, sustaining 828.5 mAh/g even after over 3200 cycles at 2 A/g [159]. The substitution of nitrogen was also explored in GDY via the Glaser-Hay coupling reaction using pentaethynylpyridine as a monomer forming a 2D nitrogen-substituted graphdiyne (PyN-GDY) [160]. When employed as a LIB anode, PyN-GDY demonstrated superior electrochemical performance, boasting excellent cycling stability and a high reversible capacity of 1450 mAh/g at 0.05 A/g. However, the reversible capacity reached 1000 mAh/g at 1 A/g and 500 mAh/g at 5 A/g.

GDY from the pyrazinoquinoxaline-based film was characterized for LIBs. In the findings, over half of the Li-ions were bound to pyrazine nitrogen, with a reversible capacity of 570 mAh/g at 200 mA/g for 800 cycles with a 97.2 % capacity utilization rate [161]. The solvothermal method was also utilized to prepare GDY hollow nanospheres, employing copper oxide nanospheres as a template and catalyst source. This excellent electrochemical performance at a high mass loading of 2.0 mg/cm2 and achieved a 1020 mAh/g capacity at 50 mA/g maintaining 94 % of the initial capacity post 8000 cycles at 2 A/g [160].

LTO anodes were also modified using GY, resulting in better electron conductivity and Li-ion hosting [155]. The LTO/GY composite achieved excellent rate capability and long-term cycling stability, retaining 83 % of its capacity at a 10C rate and 91 % over 1000 cycles at 2C (Fig. 16B). In addition, it afforded high performance when cold as evidenced by the ability to reach 102 mAh/g at −20 °C after five hundred cycles. A reduction in plateau-potential polarization and promotion of interfacial ionic migration via ordered triangle channels of γ-GY underlie this enhancement. Electrolyte decomposition and gas formation could thereby be prevented and high-temperature performance enhanced through encapsulation, which also reduced direct contact between the electrolyte and the Li4Ti5O12 electrodes. The summary of compositions, main features, and performance characteristics of different GDY-incorporated LIBs can be found in Table 8 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Consequences of compromising between fast charging and life span: towards potential commercialization
ce:section.ce:para:
On prioritizing fast charging, the cycle life is reduced because of:
1.
Accelerated degradation via increased strain and cracking: Fast charging causes rapid lithium intercalation/de-intercalation that induces significant volume changes in the carbon structure. This leads to increased mechanical stress, particle cracking, and loss of electrical contact within the electrode over repeated cycles, ultimately shortening the lifespan [163].
2.
Enhanced side reactions: High current densities due to fast charging amplifies side reactions with the electrolyte forming thick SEI layer. The thick SEI layer thus consumes more Li-ions and electrolyte, fading the capacity and increasing the impedance over cycling [163].
3.
Non-uniform lithium plating: At low temperatures with high charging rates, Li-ions might not intercalate uniformly into the carbon structure resulting in lithium plating on the anode surface. The Li plating can be a dangerous as it can cause short circuits and thermal runaway, severely impacting lifespan and safety [163–165].

To bridge the gap between fast charging and lifespan and achieve commercially viable hybrid carbon anodes, several optimization strategies can be employed:
1.
Characteristic pore structuring: Creating a multi-scale pore network that includes both macropores for efficient electrolyte transport and mesopores/micropores for high surface area and lithium storage can facilitate both fast charging and high capacity without sacrificing structural integrity [124].
2.
Forming thermodynamically stable anode interfaces: Porous carbons are mainly unstable and have poor contact at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Particularly at anode, the electrolyte interface is thermodynamically unstable and there is low ionic conductivity at the metastable interface. This leads to another issues between the SEI film and the anode [166]. Therefore, it is important to precisely control the interfaces between different carbon components can optimize electron and ion transport pathways, minimize strain during cycling, and potentially suppress side reactions. This can be achieved by ensuring robust mechanical and electrical connections between different carbon phases to prevent delamination and maintain conductivity during volume changes.
3.
Selecting the material strategically: Choosing and combining different carbon allotropes based on their individual strengths can lead to synergistic effects. Such as, utilizing graphene/CNTs for high conductivity and mechanical strength and soft/hard carbon for high capacity and controlled volume expansion [166].
4.
Advanced in-situ characterization for monitoring: To understand the degradation mechanisms and potentially implement feedback control strategies, exploring advanced characterization techniques on battery operation for optimizing charging protocols and extending lifespan.
5.
Theoretical analysis and investigation: Modeling and optimizing algorithms to explore hybrid carbon structures and identify compositions and architectures that offer the best balance between fast charging and lifespan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hybrids of lithium titanate (LTO) and carbon allotrope
ce:section.ce:para: With the theoretical capacity of 372 mAh/g for commercial graphite [167], one of the concerning issue is that at high currents Li metal dendrites grow resulting in severe safety hazards [168]. In that respect, Spinel LTO is a promising alternate anode material, showcasing its ability to operate at high charge-discharge rates, forming a stable solid electrolyte interface (eliminating Li plating risk) and is termed as “zero strain material” [169] with <0.3 % volume change during lithiation. From literature studies, LTO operates well at higher temperatures ranging from to 50–80 °C [170]. Though the high temperature is believed to improve the performance with an increased diffusion of Li-ions, constant operation leads to aging and eventually thermal runaway, in case of improper heat dissipation. Moreover, temperatures above 60 °C reduce the capacity due to gas evolution [170]. The utility of LTO is also limited by its low theoretical specific capacity (175 mAhg−1) and high operating voltage of ~1.5 V (vs Li/Li+) that reduce the energy density of LTO-based LIBs compared to those achievable with graphite anodes [171].

For comparison, LTO and graphite were applied one after the other by Xu et al. as the anode and obtained that LTO has 20 kJ/mol lower energy barrier for Li-ion charge transfer than graphite anode. The shift in the values was attributed to the absence of an SEI layer resulting in the capability of LTO for fast charging/discharging applications compared to graphite [172].

As the electrical conductivity of graphite and LTO are (2-1) × 103 S/cm and 10−9–10−13 S/cm, respectively [173,174], to form a composite, Feng-ling et al. [175] synthesized graphite oxide via Hummer's method, with nanoparticles generated inside through ball-milling method, and fabricated nano-LTO/rGO samples. The sample with an optimum composition was labeled as LTO/GO-1.75 with graphene content of 13.7 %. According to XRD results, the layer distance indicated that graphite oxide cannot be exfoliated by weak mechanical force even in the sufficient expansion of layer distance, the existence of this peak reveals the exfoliation of graphene layers. TEM and HRTEM also confirmed the size range of 15–20 nm for deposited nano-LTO particles, and 0.48 nm lattice spacing related to the crystal plane (111) of spinel LTO. The electrochemical measurements revealed 163 mAh/g for the initial capacity, and 136 mAh/g for the specific capacity even at 7000 mA/g, leading to high capacity retention of 81 %, representing a good cycle performance. Moreover, after 1000 cycles at 7000 mA/g, 72 % capacity retention was reported, and the efficiency of each cycle was reached to nearly 100 %. Owing to tap density, 4.66 × 10−3 mol/cm3 was gained for the molar concentrations. The values of Rs (Ω), Rct (Ω), σ (Ω/s1/2), and D (cm2/s) related to the optimum sample were 2.6, 40.4, 53.9, and 2.11 × 10−11, respectively.

Recently, Chen et al. [176] worked on 2D graphene-based spinel LTO in which GO and TiO2/rGO were synthesized through the modified Hummer's method and the hydrolysis of tetrabutyltitanate (Ti(OC4H9)4) on the graphene, correspondingly. The amorphous TiO2/rGO sample was prepared by using 0.1 M LiOH, (HP-LTO/rGO-1). Galvanostatic charge/discharge measure was used for the cycle and rate performance of the sample and illustrated (1) decreases in capacity the initial several cycles, (2) capacity remains relatively stable after some cycles, (3) capacity reaches to 206 mAh/g after 500 cycles; higher than that of pure LTO which is 175 mAh/g, and (4) capacity in ultra-high rate i.e., 10C, maintained at 168 mAh/g after 1000 cycles; reflecting a good cycle performance. Pseudocapacitive behavior was also observed in this sample, which is highly associated with its particle size and specific surface area. Moreover, cycling stability was concluded due to absence of particle agglomeration and pulverization. An increase in the rate from 1C to 30C, reduction in capacity from 215 mAh/g (after 6 cycles) to 125 mAh/g (after 36 cycles). An improvement in the electron and Li+ transfer and consequently rate performance was attributed to the graphene substrate and small LTO nanoparticles assembly with a hierarchical pore structure.

To improve the conductivity of LTO, one of the promising techniques was to make a hybrid of CNT and LTO. The composite structure of LTO and CNT retained its droplet structure [177], favorable for LTO packing, resulting in high-rate performance of the sample from 1C to 30C. LTO-CNT composites synthesized through solid state sand milling [178] and other techniques [179,180], displayed superior electrochemical performances and higher rate performances than pure LTO.

Cao et al. [181] fabricated perovskite (Na0.35La0.55TiO3) embedded in multichannel carbon fibers (NLTO-CNF) is rationally designed and synthesized through the electrospinning method. This NLTO-CNF anode delivered excellent comprehensive electrochemical performances, including a high reversible specific capacity of 265 mAh/g at 0.1 A/g, good rate capability, and ultralong cycling stability with 96.3 % capacity retention after 9000 cycles at 2 A/g. On the basis of the in-situ XRD analysis, only 0.76 % of the maximum unit-cell volume change confirms the representative “zero-strain” characteristic. Kar et al. [182] also assembled a micro single-fiber battery using LTO coated on CNFs as anodes that delivered a higher power density (~0.0312 W/cm3) and energy density comparable to solid-state thin-film lithium-ion batteries (~0.006 W h/cm3).

Therefore, the hybridization of LTO at normal/elevated temperatures with carbon allotropes like graphene and CNTs can effectively mitigate LTO's low conductivity and enhance its rate performance and cycling stability, as evidenced by improved capacity retention and higher capacities even at high current densities. These composites combine the synergistic effects of structural stability of LTO at high temperature and conductivity of carbon allotropes with high conductivity to overcome the limitations of pure LTO for fast-charging LIB applications.

The lifespan of LIBs is closely tied to their charging capacity, as illustrated in Fig. 17 . Batteries typically undergo degradation in electric vehicles until they reach approximately 80 % of their capacity, further when charging capacity declines to around 70 % in their second life, these batteries are redirected for stationary use [183,184]. As the charge capacity falls to or below 30 %, they are then designated for recycling. With the current focus on recycling only cathode materials, anode materials (such as graphite) are disregarded from the perspective due to their low cost and return on investment (ROI). However, disposing of anode carbon materials with procedures like landfill incineration substantially increases carbon emission and creates waste and needs to be addressed.

In the context of formulating strategies for a circular economy aimed at sustainable development, the recycling of carbon materials assumes paramount importance. The recycling process typically involves disassembling the spent LIBs to separate anode components from the other parts. Post separation, mechanical milling/grinding or exfoliation of the carbon anode is followed especially for reducing particle sizes. A high-temperature thermal treatment is further conducted to remove binder materials. The last step is decided by the purification method that is best suited to retain the properties of the carbon allotrope. For example, chemical oxidation and reduction purification can be chosen for graphene. Fullerenes can be treated with a preferentially solvent extraction method. The table below highlights the purification method related to carbon allotropes. As the first three steps remain the same for all the allotropes in the subsequent sections, we will focus on purification methods and their relevance.

Graphite i.e. predominantly used as an anode material, holds critical importance due to its ubiquity and functional merits such as superior conductivity, stability, and cost-effectiveness [185]. Graphite recycling encompasses sophisticated pretreatment procedures to detach graphite from the copper or aluminum foil substrates using technologies like pyrometallurgy. This method involves subjecting the anode materials to high temperatures that oxidize the carbonaceous matter, leaving a residue composed primarily of metal oxides. Hydrometallurgical techniques can further process these oxides to extract valuable metals like lithium and cobalt [186]. Despite its potential, the process is challenged by the degradation of graphite's structural integrity, induced by the high temperatures involved, which significantly diminishes its recyclability [186].

Innovative approaches such as hybrid heating with microwave radiation are being explored to mitigate these thermal challenges. This method leverages microwave radiation's rapid, localized heating capabilities to enhance graphite recycling efficiency by lowering necessary temperatures and simplifying the separation of materials [187]. Additionally, various pre-treatment methods are utilized to recycle graphite anodes, including pyrolysis, hydrometallurgy, supercritical, and water treatment, each contributing to the efficient separation and purification of graphite from other battery components [185]. Mechanical processes such as grinding and sieving play a crucial role in separating the graphite from its substrate, followed by hydrometallurgical processes to recover valuable metals like lithium and cobalt [186].

Using the above procedures, the resulting purified graphite is ready for integration into new anode materials for LIBs. The recycling approach for graphite offers notable advantages. Firstly, graphite exhibits excellent stability and cycling performance, making it a highly desirable material for battery applications [25]. In recent years, some novel graphite direct recycling technologies have gradually emerged. Deep eutectic solvents (DES) have shown great promise in the direct recycling of graphite anodes. This innovative method treated spent graphite from LIBs with DES to remove impurities and regenerate high-purity graphite. The process involved leaching spent graphite with DES, stirring for several hours, followed by centrifugation and repeated washes with deionized water. The graphite is then dried to achieve a purity of 98.9 %, closely matching that of commercial graphite. The regenerated graphite demonstrates excellent electrochemical performance in half-cells, maintaining robust capacity after many cycles (Fig. 18A) [188].

In another approach, spent graphite is treated in an acid bath, followed by a thorough cleaning with DMC solution, alcohol, and deionized water. The graphite is then separated from copper foil, dried, and subjected to acid leaching and a high-temperature carbon-thermal reduction process under nitrogen to reconstruct its structure. Using this approach for recycling the graphite managed to retain 75 % of its capacity after a thousand cycles, attributed to an optimized dense SEI surface [129]. An eco-friendly method was also adopted to regenerate graphite from used LIBs, where EIS was performed to evaluate the conductivity, Li-ion diffusivity, and structural stability of recycled graphite, achieving almost 361 mAh/g at 1C over 100 cycles [189]. The process involved immersing spent LIB in sodium chloride solution to discharge it, followed by air drying and disassembly, then high-temperature smelting of the anode under a nitrogen atmosphere to remove organics and volatilize lithium, and finally, ultrasonication and sieving to recover and prepare the graphite for further use (Fig. 18B). As a more sustainable approach to graphite utilization, water leaching can be utilized to separate graphite from impurities and copper foil. This is followed by drying and dual-stage purification—initially heating under an inert argon atmosphere and then treating with dimethyl carbonate (DMC) before a second heating phase—they enhance the degree of graphitization. This process not only recovers high-performance graphite but also minimizes environmental impact by using less harsh chemicals and lower temperatures compared to traditional methods [190].

The spent graphite was also transformed into regenerated graphite with superior electrochemical performance compared to commercial materials by high-temperature shock. Mainly due to the ultrafast heating and cooling that restored the graphite layer structure [191]. Apart from the above-mentioned methods-electrochemical methods, microwave calcination, and subcritical CO2 extraction have also been explored for direct graphite recycling [192].

Recycling graphite mitigates the need for extensive mining and processing of raw graphite resources, particularly while reducing the environmental footprint of battery production. Incorporating recycled graphite into new batteries can save costs and conserve resources [25]. Despite the benefits of the recycling strategies, technical hurdles in recycling hinder its practical applications. For example, the involved mechanical milling process can introduce defects and alter the structure of the graphite, affecting its electrochemical performance [193]. Completely removing binder materials during thermal treatment can be challenging, indicating that it may still have impurities that impact battery efficiency [193]. In addition, the economic viability of recycling graphite remains contentious. The costs associated with the advanced recycling processes often exceed those for new graphite production, raising concerns about the practicality of these recycling methods in commercial applications [185,186]. Particularly, the scale-up of such recycling technologies faces hurdles due to technological limitations and economic constraints [185,186,194]. Another challenge with anode recycling is that the graphite particles can become contaminated with metals and other impurities during the cycling process, which can affect the performance of the recycled anode material.

Nevertheless, the environmental impact of extracting graphite, coupled with the increasing demand for LIBs, obviously underscores the need for effective recycling technologies that can support sustainable battery production [195,196]. Effective graphite recycling not only conserves natural resources but also reduces the ecological footprint of battery manufacturing, aligning with broader environmental goals and regulations [194].

Interestingly, a recent study offered a novel method for recycling spent LIBs, focusing on extracting high-quality graphene and lithium from spent graphite. By recharging LIBs to form lithium graphite intercalation compounds and employing hydrolysis and ultrasonic treatment, the study successfully produced graphene with minimal defects, while simultaneously recovering lithium [197]. This greener and more sustainable approach contrasts sharply with conventional methods, such as chemical oxidation/reduction or pyrometallurgy, which often rely on harsh chemicals and high temperatures. Instead, this method leverages milder conditions to efficiently separate and recover materials, transforming spent graphite into lithium-graphite intercalation compounds that facilitate the exfoliation of graphite into high-quality graphene during hydrolysis and ultrasonic treatment. This method provides an efficient, sustainable solution for recycling spent graphite and recovering valuable materials.

Researchers have identified the reuse potential of graphite from spent LIBs as a resource for efficient graphene production. After undergoing multiple charge-discharge cycles, this graphite is more prone to exfoliation compared to natural graphite, enhancing the yield and quality of graphene. The process typically involves simple detachment of graphite powder from the anode, coupled with innovative methods like electrochemical exfoliation, which collectively contribute to sustainable and high-quality graphene synthesis (Fig. 19A) [197,199,200].

They used a high-energy ball milling process as a green and efficient approach. They also incorporated nitrogen into the graphite's structure, which significantly improved the discharge capacity and stability in comparison to commercial graphite [201]. In another study, graphite powders from end-of-life LIB anodes were utilized to fabricate graphene and graphene oxide [198]. This process, involved shear mixing and an H2SO4 treatment, significantly improving the productivity of graphene up to four times compared to that derived from pristine graphite. The treatment not only enhanced the lattice structure of the graphite but also resulted in graphene sheets with intact crystal structure, suitable for reinforcing materials like epoxy. The approach integrates seamlessly into existing recycling processes, offering a low-cost and comprehensive solution for battery recycling while efficiently producing high-quality graphene (Fig. 19B).

AFM inspection (Fig. 19B(i)) and thickness distribution measurements showed that most obtained graphene sheets were thin, with less than seven atomic layers, further confirming the effectiveness of the H2SO4 treatment in producing high-quality graphene [198]. After shear mixing, the graphene suspension from H2SO4-treated anode graphite displayed no notable separation even after 120 h, with 83.7 % and 63.3 % graphene productivity after 48 and 120 h, respectively, which is significantly higher than that of the untreated anode and pristine graphite.

Since only graphite is commercially available as an anode and other carbons are yet to reach commercialization, major efforts are focused on graphite cycling. Apart from the initial recycling steps, different purification steps can be employed for other carbons as stated in Table 9 [200,202]. For instance, to purify and functionalize graphene, chemical processes including oxidation and reduction are generally employed [203,204]. On the other hand, fullerene purification is achieved using solvent extraction for selective recovery and refinement of fullerene derivatives [205]. In the subsequent steps, chromatography or crystallization further isolates and refines fullerene [206]. For CNTs, the purification is done via a multi-step process mainly utilizing acid treatments or thermal oxidation [207–209]. Such recycling practices help conserve resources by reducing the need for new raw materials [210]. Similar to the above carbons for purification, CNWs also undergo chemical processes, including acid treatment or plasma oxidation, to eliminate the residual binder materials and enhance purity [211,212].

Recycling carbon nanowalls from LIBs offers compelling advantages, including recycling CNWs eases the demand for new nanomaterial synthesis, reducing the environmental impact of their production [211]. The recycling process for CSP-based anode materials follows a multi-step extraction, refining, and reintegrating approach [213]. Like CNWs, CNPs undergo acid treatment and thermal oxidation [214,215]. Graphdiyne also follows the same approach [216], though differs in chemical processes, such as oxidative treatments or functionalization reactions for purification. The abovementioned purified carbons can be integrated or utilized to synthesize new electrode materials, thus enhancing LIB production sustainability. Recycling carbon allotropes is believed to lower the demand for novel nanomaterial synthesis, address resource scarcity concerns, and eliminate additional production's environmental and economic impacts [217]. However, mechanical methods like ball milling can introduce defects and alter the carbon structure, such as in CNTs, affecting their electrochemical properties. Purification steps may also impact the quality and structure of carbon allotropes, necessitating careful optimization. Accordingly, the process is advantageous for the environment but affects quality, an inevitable tradeoff requiring additional research and improvement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural degradation and SEI contamination in spent carbon anodes
ce:section.ce:para: Spent LIBs anodes based on graphite suffer structural and interfacial degradation, which complicates their recycling. Graphite is prone to microcracking, exfoliation, and loss of crystallinity for intermediated lithium intercalation cycles [223,224]. Co-intercalation solvents can also delaminate layers and expand graphite, exposing new defect sites that accelerate SEI formation [224] The SEI which consists of Li₂O, LiF, Li₂CO₃, and ROCO₂Li forms a passivating film but captures lithium, increases impedance, and becomes unstable during cycling [224,225]. Aged graphite is estimated to retain 3–5 wt% lithium due to SEI and intercalated species [226], rendering it less pure. Fast charging is liable to induce lithium plating, weakening SEI and increasing recycling difficulty [224]. The presence of conductive additives like carbon black and binders such as PVDF makes separation more complicated. The thermal stability of PVDF hinders graphite-Cu foil delamination [185]. Early laboratory-scale work demonstrated that PVDF could be dissolved by N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP), which supports solvent-based binder removal methods employed in contemporary recycling approaches [227]. While shredding achieves coarse material recovery, electrochemical reuse is hindered by residual binder and SEI. During thermal treatment, graphite foil and PVDF in conjunction with electrolytes can decompose to form HF gas, posing a risk due to toxicity [228]. Thus, recycling is challenged by (i) conductivity-disrupting damage, (ii) lithium-trapping SEI, and (iii) persistent organic residues [225]. In addition, microcracks worsen SEI regrowth, increasing lithium depletion, under high current density, leading to dendrite formation that can pierce SEI and separator risking short circuits [224].

Several recycling methods have been developed to address these issues starting with thermal treatments. Thermal treatments between 400 and 500 °C are used to remove organics and separate graphite from Cu foil [226]. Yang et al. demonstrated the staged heating method for binder and metal residue removal [226]. Though effective, pyrolysis can cause structural defects, as evidenced by SEM-observed morphological changes [229]. Roasting can also oxidize Lithium residues to Li₂CO₃ while graphitizing to some extent, which is done at up to 1000 °C [225]. While thermal methods are effective for detoxifying, they also raise defect density which increases irreversible capacity from SEI reformation [226]. The application of acids such as HCl or citric acid helps to remove the SEI layer while still keeping the graphite intact. As shown by Guo et al., over 99 % lithium removal was achieved with 3 M HCl at 80 °C [225], and later Yang et al. used citric acid to regain a capacity of 330 mAh·g−1 with a Coulombic efficiency surpassing 99 % [226]. Water leaching also removed 87 % lithium at ambient temperature [225]. These approaches enhance purity and capacity [223]. Citric acid leaching (90 °C, 0.2 mol·L−1) was confirmed by Zhao and group to remove over 97 % lithium while maintaining overall conductivity [230]. A combined pyrolysis–carbonated water–acid leaching method was demonstrated by Ren et al. to reclaim 87.9 % lithium while counteracting 99.1 % cobalt with Ca(OH)₂ that sequestered HF emissions [231]. This integrated approach optimally combines these practices with minimal costs to the natural environment.

Further, to improve the durability, catalytic graphitization was used to restore the structure using cobalt or iron at graphite temperatures between 600 and 3000 °C. Such as Chen et al. achieved 245 mAh/g over 500 cycles after reducing defect ratios from 0.449 to 0.244 [5]. In addition, Ruan et al. also demonstrated recovery of charge capacities of 334–344 mAh/g with over 95 % retention over 50 cycles after performing thermal and acid leaching followed by graphitization [224]. Also using certain graphite-free microwave techniques, PVDF/SEI can be removed within 30 s to regenerate graphite can achieve 438 mAh/g in the first cycle [230]. Surface engineering further enhances the electrochemical performance of recycled anodes. For instance, applying a 10 % carbon coating to regenerated graphite has been shown to increase first-cycle efficiency to approximately 91 % and improve capacity retention to 96.6 %, reflecting the formation of a robust conductive interface that stabilizes lithium intercalation [224]. As graphite remains the primary commercial anode for Li-ion batteries, most current research has focused on recycling strategies for graphite recovery from spent LIBs. However, other carbon-based anode materials—such as CNTs, CNFs, and graphene present distinct recycling challenges. Their high electrical conductivity and nanoscale architecture make them prone to agglomeration within polymeric binders and lead to the entrapment of SEI layers. In the absence of tailored separation or regeneration methods, these materials are typically co-processed or incinerated at elevated temperatures, which can deteriorate their structural integrity and electrochemical performance [230]. While purification processes, for instance, thermal annealing, acid leaching, and mechanical reconditioning effectively remove binders and SEI films, they may compromise the structural integrity of delicate nanocarbons like CNTs, CNWs, and CSPs. Harsh treatment procedures such as high-temperature oxidation and strong acidic processes can introduce defects, reduce aspect ratios, and degrade conductivity, ultimately impacting electrochemical performance. Consequently, recycling strategies must balance effective impurity removal while maintaining the structure and functionality of the nanocarbon material [231].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing alternative carbon allotropes (e.g., CNTs, graphene, fullerenes) with graphite in terms of cost, performance, stability, and recyclability
ce:section.ce:para: Graphite anodes have remained unsurpassed in their industry dominance due to their low cost and excellent cycling stability [11]. Other carbon allotropes, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, improve electrochemical performance; however, trade-offs limit practicality. Nanostructured carbons exceed graphite's capacity of 372 mAh/g for instance, carboxylated C₆₀ achieved around 861 mAh/g [12]. However, with higher surface area and conductivity, CNTs and graphene boost rate capability and capacity, but the practicality is greatly hindered. Production of graphene and CNTs is far more costly than mined or synthetic graphite due to the energy-intense CVD or arc-discharge processes used for production [13]. These increases in material costs would directly impact the total expense of the battery. The same applies to C₆₀, which are also extraordinarily expensive. Although, after chemical modification of C₆₀ remarkable capacity and rate performance can be achieved [12], their intricate synthesis and scarcity renders the practical use of fullerenes for large-scale applications.

With regard to cycling stability, many of the nanocarbon anodes face some issues. Excessive lithium loss and SEI formation are problems due to high surface area. Individual carbon nanowalls (vertical graphene) possess fast kinetic properties, however, if used alone, they are prone to collapse or delaminate during repetitive cycling, which undermines longevity [13]. Carbon nanofibers or other similar scaffolds will enhance the mechanical properties but increase the steps for synthesis [12]. Certain emerging allotropes do exhibit remarkable structural stability such as graphdiyne-based anodes whose capacity retention showed only minimal fade over long cycling [14]. However, these materials (graphyne/graphdiyne) are still laboratory interests, synthesized in small batches at great expense. Carbon submicron spheres (hard carbon microspheres, etc.) can enhance electrode packing and, in some cases, cycle stability, but they commonly contain elaborate preparation (e.g., templating or pyrolysis) and haven't demonstrated a significant performance gain over graphite in commercial cells.

Another critical issue is recyclability. Present battery recycling techniques rarely recover the anode carbon, graphite is frequently treated as low-value waste and burned off [10,11]. Replacing graphite with superior carbon nanomaterials could therefore exacerbate end-of-life costs unless recycling processes advance to recover them. In summary, while alternative carbon allotropes are able to enhance Li-ion battery performance (e.g., higher capacity or power), their high production costs, sometimes mixed cycling results, and uncertain recyclability significantly limit their practicality relative to cheap, robust graphite [10]. Each novel carbon will require not only superior electrochemical traits but also solutions for scalable manufacturing and sustainable end-of-life management before it can viably displace graphite in LIB anodes.

From the comprehensive electrochemical evaluations across diverse carbon allotropes presented in this review, it is evident that while graphite remains the benchmark for commercial LIB anodes due to its high cycling stability and acceptable capacity about 372 mAh/g, alternative allotropes offer promising but nuanced advantages. Graphene, for instance, demonstrates superior electron mobility and 744 mAh/g theoretical capacities, especially when incorporated into hybrid frameworks or doped to modulate surface chemistry. CNTs present rapid ion transport due to their 1D hollow structure and high conductivity, although challenges such as metallic impurities and high synthesis costs remain limiting. Fullerenes, particularly functionalized C₆₀, have shown extraordinary reversible capacities, 861 mAh/g through enhanced lithium complexation mechanisms, but suffer from poor scalability and electrolyte compatibility. CNWs and nanofibers exhibit enhanced rate capabilities due to their exposed active sites and conductive pathways yet face stability concerns without structural reinforcement. Emerging forms like graphdiyne exhibit high Li-ion adsorption and low diffusion barriers, outperforming traditional carbons in theoretical metrics, though they remain limited to lab-scale fabrication. Across all these allotropes, common electrochemical improvements such as enhanced rate performance, higher capacity, and tailored lithiation pathways are often offset by real-world challenges including initial Coulombic inefficiency, rapid SEI formation, and manufacturing constraints. Therefore, while alternative carbon materials offer localized improvements, none yet surpass graphite in the balance of cost, electrochemical durability, and integration readiness. For meaningful commercial deployment, these materials must demonstrate sustained high-rate performance, minimize irreversible capacity losses, and be paired with scalable synthesis and end-of-life recovery pathways.

We focus on creating a framework to recognize the best-suited carbon allotropes as anodes in LIBs by leveraging theoretical insights and electrochemical investigations. Within this framework, the recyclability of carbon anodes is also included to enable sustainability as a primary goal in the deployment of these carbons for practical purposes. The theoretical studies mainly elaborated on the atomic/molecular level interaction of Li-ions with carbon. This CLi interaction is mostly measured using simulated diffusion coefficients of ions, binding ability (Li adsorption energy), electron mobility, and activation energy barriers. The above parameters differ for each carbon based on their surface area, presence of defects, edges and vacancies, electrode composition, and interface properties. These insights support improving the electrochemical performance of LIBs. For instance, the presence of defects in graphene inhibited cluster formation leading to efficient Li-ion adsorption, and the graphene layer stacking created a significant difference in the diffusion of Li-ions through them. In addition to the detailed interaction of Li with each carbon, the electrochemical section described the performance of LIBs using different carbon anodes from electrochemical parameters. It highlights the challenges observed with the carbon structures in LIBs and identifies approaches adopted by far. Specifically, modification of graphene structure to Li4Ti5O12 graphene foam enabled fast charging with a rate retention of up to 200 °C in 18 s. Fast charging was also achieved in graphite when the hard carbon/graphite ratio was optimized. Many such examples where the hybridization of carbons with different materials resolved issues like improved kinetics, volume expansion, high capacity, rate capability, and stability are discussed briefly in each section of carbon allotropes. From a sustainability perspective, the review envisioned the recyclability of each carbon allotrope by presenting various recycling methods, primarily associated with the purification step best suited for each allotrope. This section examined the electrochemical performance of recycled carbon anodes, emphasizing their efficiency in battery performance and the environmental impact of additional carbon production.

Looking towards sustainable developments of LIBs, future research should focus on developing new types of carbon allotropes, such as carbon aerogels, carbon quantum dots, and graphene oxide, tailoring the structures of existing ones, combining these allotropes with other materials, creating scalable production methods, and examining their long-term stability. Progress in these areas could lead to improved battery performance and longer cycle life, making LIBs more suitable for a wider range of applications. Apart from the development of new types of carbon-based electrodes, a detailed discussion on the recyclable use of LIB materials emphasizes considering material recyclability as a prerequisite in choosing electrode materials. Typically, carbon materials utilized as anode contribute to lower carbon emissions. However, the complex designs of battery packs and modules consisting of cathode/anode materials make them difficult to separate and open. Therefore, easy designs must be considered for efficient operation and recycling. On disassembling the batteries, the use of binders and adhesives primarily governs the recovery of active electrode material. Water-soluble binders/adhesives can improve the recovery efficiency. Water-soluble binders enhance LIB recyclability by enabling facile disassembly of electrodes through water immersion, eliminating toxic solvents like NMP and reducing energy consumption compared to high-temperature PVDF decomposition [232,233]. To improve the recycling efficiency, the water soluble binder/adhesives should allow rapid and complete dissolution of the binder to enable efficient and high yield separation of active material from current collector, facilitate strong yet reversible adhesion to allow easy delamination upon water immersion to avoid harsh treatments for recovery of active materials, leave minimum residue on the active materials to preserve the quality and electrochemical properties of carbon materials and be nontoxic and environmentally friendly to support a sustainable recycling process.

Such as highlighted by Rudolph et al. [234] wherein they mentioned that the presence of a water-soluble anode binder, such as carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR), or polyacrylic acid (PAA), helped in delamination of graphite from the Cu foil and its disaggregation. Also, the pH of water after the operation remained neutral (ca. 7), while graphite particles appeared to be well liberated. Also, Paul et al. also mentioned that on the anode side, most water-soluble binders such as SBR are easier to separate from copper [235].

Prior to the above, as batteries in different applications have different chemistries, labeling of battery packs with their chemical composition is necessary but often neglected. This induces cross-contamination and hurdles to the separation of battery components. From another perspective, it is also important to consider the carbon emission involved in the recycling process. Labeling and highlighting the recyclability and carbon emissions of the battery product will promote transparency in the specs and encourage its environmental feasibility as a selling point. To achieve a balance between environmental sustainability and economic viability, it is essential to undertake a comprehensive investigation and optimization of battery design and recycling processes. This will pave the way for the sustainable reuse of LIB materials.
